selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically N-0 neck
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a better alternative to conventional imaging for nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: In the clinically N-0 neck, PET does not appear to be better than conventional imaging, because of an unacceptably high false-negative rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically accessible site of suspected recurrence or surveillance after treatment
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on PET for assessment because accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to recurrence and tumour surveillance after treatment, the evidence suggests that sites of disease that are clinically accessible for assessment did not benefit from PET imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal, or treatment may be significantly modified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for M staging and bilateral nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in the M and bilateral nodal staging of all patients with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma where conventional imaging is equivocal, or where treatment may be significantly modified.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Primary site is unknown after conventional imaging
selected_rules.execute: Use PET after conventional imaging and before or in addition to diagnostic panendoscopy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in all patients after conventional imaging and in addition to, or prior to, diagnostic panendoscopy where the primary site is unknown.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cervical lymph node metastases and the primary site is unknown after conventional imaging or clinical examination
selected_rules.execute: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor
selected_rules.source_statement: One primary study indicated that patients with cervical metastasis and an unknown primary site after undergoing conventional imaging or clinical examination benefit from PET/CT prior to panendoscopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal for recurrence assessment
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for assessment of recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Need to detect distant metastatic disease in head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use PET because it is better than conventional imaging for distant metastasis detection
selected_rules.source_statement: There is strong evidence that PET imaging is valuable in detecting distant metastatic disease and is better than conventional imaging.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for physical examination and biopsy. The clinician is considering PET/CT to look for nodal disease and to help localize a primary site before panendoscopy.
recommended_actions: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor in a patient with cervical lymph node metastases and an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination.
Do not rely on PET for assessment of the accessible neck mass because clinically accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the recommendation to use PET/CT before panendoscopy because the patient has cervical nodal metastasis with an unknown primary after conventional imaging and exam. It also triggers the caution against relying on PET for assessment of a clinically accessible site, since the palpable neck mass is directly examinable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is HER2-positive
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive ... breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is triple negative
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive or triple negative breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering multiple multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for one treatment decision
selected_rules.execute: Do not order multiple different multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for a single decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Requesting multiple tests with different multigene profiling assays on an individual tumour specimen to guide a single treatment decision is discouraged.

selected_rules.activation_condition: node-positive ER-positive HER2-negative breast cancer with higher risk features and considering withholding adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, or Breast Cancer Index to justify withholding adjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, and Breast Cancer Index assays can identify low-risk node-positive patients whose prognostic outcomes are favourable; however, these assays have not demonstrated predictive evidence to support withholding adjuvant chemotherapy among higher risk, node-positive, ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, or EndoPredict to extend adjuvant endocrine therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use these assays to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, and EndoPredict currently have insufficient evidence to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has lymph node-negative disease or 1-3 positive lymph nodes and is being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy, with supporting clinical/pathological/patient factors
selected_rules.execute: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Multigene profiling assays are recommended for use in patients with lymph node-negative or lymph node-positive (1-3 lymph nodes) disease who are under consideration for adjuvant chemotherapy if the use is supported by other clinical, pathological, or patient-related factors.

selected_rules.activation_condition: one multigene profiling assay is being ordered for a patient
selected_rules.execute: Request only one assay per patient for a specific treatment decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: One multigene profiling assay should be requested per patient to guide a specific treatment decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and systemic therapy decision is needed
selected_rules.execute: Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with early-stage estrogen receptor (ER)-positive/human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2)-negative breast cancer, clinicians should consider using multigene profiling assays ... to help guide the use of systemic therapy.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 2 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team plans to use the result for this single treatment decision and does not intend to send multiple different assays on the same specimen.
recommended_actions: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision, since she has node-positive disease with 1-3 positive nodes and relevant clinical/pathological factors.
Request only one multigene profiling assay for this specific treatment decision.
Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy in this early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer case.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer with 1-3 positive nodes and a real adjuvant chemotherapy decision, which supports use of a multigene profiling assay. The rules also specify that only one assay should be ordered for a single treatment decision. The case does not trigger any rule against assay use, because it is not HER2-positive, triple-negative, or a scenario of withholding chemotherapy based on a low-risk result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed biopsy-confirmed prostate cancer; intermediate- or high-risk; being considered for radical therapy; knowledge of extraprostatic extension would alter management
selected_rules.execute: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
selected_rules.source_statement: Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) use for pre-treatment local staging of prostate cancer is a reasonable option for assessment of extraprostatic extension (EPE) in intermediate-and highrisk patients being considered for radical therapy if knowledge of EPE will alter management.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team notes that knowing whether there is extraprostatic extension would affect the surgical plan and whether nerve-sparing could be attempted. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
recommended_actions: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
generated_case.rationale: This is a newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed intermediate-risk prostate cancer case being considered for radical therapy, and knowledge of possible extraprostatic extension would change management. That matches the rule’s activation condition, supporting pre-treatment mpMRI for local staging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer for whom radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with cervical cancer for whom treatment with radiotherapy is being considered (described above) should be offered concurrent cisplatin with their course of radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer receiving cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or hydroxyurea
selected_rules.execute: Use concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy because pooled survival favored this approach.
selected_rules.source_statement: Pooled survival rates detected a statistically significant effect in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or with hydroxyurea (relative risk of death, 0.74; 95% confidence interval, 0.64 to 0.86).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk of death among the six trials that enrolled only women with locally advanced cervical cancer was 0.78 (95% confidence interval, 0.67 to 0.90) in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy and radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk for the two trials in high-risk early-stage disease also demonstrated a significant benefit for the addition of cisplatin-based chemotherapy to radiotherapy (relative risk, 0.56; 95% confidence interval, 0.41 to 0.77).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with bulky clinical stage IB cervical cancer (>4 cm) who are treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with bulky clinical stage IB (>4 cm) cervical cancer, who are treated with radiotherapy,

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer after hysterectomy who will be treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer (node-positive or margin-positive), who will be treated with radiotherapy following hysterectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with locally advanced cervical cancer,

selected_rules.activation_condition: When choosing a cisplatin regimen for concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 based on available toxicity data.
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the review of the available toxicity data from the randomized controlled trials, the Disease Site Group felt that cisplatinum should be given weekly (40 mg/m2).

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 5.5 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension but no distant metastases, consistent with locally advanced disease. Her oncology team is planning definitive pelvic radiotherapy with brachytherapy. She has adequate renal function and performance status to receive chemotherapy. The patient asks whether anything should be added to radiation to improve outcomes.
recommended_actions: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy for locally advanced cervical cancer.
Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 when selecting a concurrent cisplatin regimen.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette describes a woman with cervical cancer in whom radiotherapy is being considered, which triggers the general recommendation to add concurrent cisplatin. Because her disease is locally advanced and primary treatment is radiotherapy, the locally advanced cervical cancer rule also applies. The weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 option is included as a conditional regimen consideration based on available toxicity data.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage II colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of adjuvant chemotherapy for all patients with stage II colon cancer is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient without high-risk features who does not require adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: In stage II (in the absence of high-risk features) where a patient does not require adjuvant chemotherapy, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended as it will not influence that decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II colon cancer with MSI/dMMR after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Do not give fluoropyrimidine monotherapy as adjuvant treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Adjuvant chemotherapy with a fluoropyrimidine monotherapy regimen following surgery in patients who have MSI/dMMR is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage II colon cancer treated with adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine.
selected_rules.source_statement: When treated with adjuvant therapy, high-risk stage II patients should receive a fluoropyrimidine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform MSI/dMMR testing.
selected_rules.source_statement: MSI/dMMR testing should be performed for all stage II patients for whom adjuvant chemotherapy is being considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage III colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use an oxaliplatin-containing regimen such as FOLFOX, FLOX, or XELOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: These oxaliplatin-containing regimens have demonstrated superior overall survival when compared with 5-FU plus LV and are the recommended regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: low-risk stage III colon cancer (T1-3 N1)
selected_rules.execute: Prefer 3 months of CAPOX over FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: Low-risk stage III (T1-3 N1): 3 months of CAPOX is preferred over FOLFOX.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage III colon cancer (T4 +/- N2)
selected_rules.execute: Give 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy with CAPOX or FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk stage III (T4 +/-N2): 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy (CAPOX or FOLFOX).

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management.
recommended_actions: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision because the patient has stage II disease without high-risk features and the result would not influence the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
generated_case.rationale: This is a resected stage II colon cancer case without high-risk features, so routine adjuvant chemotherapy is not recommended. Because he does not require adjuvant chemotherapy based on the available features, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended for this decision.

selected_rules.execute: Perform digital rectal examination (DRE) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He has no known urinary infection symptoms and has not had any recent treatment for urinary issues. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness; no prior PSA testing has been done.
recommended_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette includes lower urinary tract symptoms in a man aged over 40, which triggers the recommendation for DRE. LUTS also supports a discussion about PSA testing with consideration of individual risk. No features of metastatic prostate cancer, abnormal incidental PSA, erectile dysfunction alone, or unexplained hematuria were included, so those rules were not triggered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy

selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and ADT, brachytherapy boost (LDR or HDR) should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving low-dose rate brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use iodine-125 or palladium-103 as reasonable isotope options.
selected_rules.source_statement: 125 I and 103 Pd are each reasonable isotope options for patients receiving LDR brachytherapy;

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma presents after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and, after discussing his options, says he prefers active treatment rather than surveillance because of significant anxiety about living with untreated cancer. He is otherwise healthy and eligible for local therapy. In clinic, he asks about surgery versus radiation and wants a clear explanation of the available approaches before deciding. The team reviews his case in a multidisciplinary setting and discusses the main treatment choices with him in a balanced way.
recommended_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and radical prostatectomy as treatment options for this eligible patient with low-risk prostate cancer.
Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette fits newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment in an eligible patient, triggering the recommendation to offer LDR brachytherapy alone, EBRT alone, and/or radical prostatectomy. It also involves counseling about management options, supporting a balanced multidisciplinary discussion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Doppler technology alone for adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Doppler technology alone to evaluate the mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evaluation of an adnexal mass by Doppler technology alone is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering serum CA-125 as a stand-alone test
selected_rules.execute: Do not use CA-125 alone to distinguish benign from malignant adnexal masses.
selected_rules.source_statement: As a stand-alone modality, serum CA-125 is not recommended for distinguishing between benign and malignant adnexal masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult woman with a suspicious isolated ovarian mass and ultrasound is technically feasible
selected_rules.execute: Use transvaginal sonography as the first imaging modality.
selected_rules.source_statement: Transvaginal sonography should be the first modality of choice, where technically feasible, in patients with a suspicious, isolated ovarian mass.

selected_rules.activation_condition: need to differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses
selected_rules.execute: Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging for differentiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Sonography, particularly three-dimensional (3D) sonography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computerized tomography (CT) imaging are each recommended for differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: ultrasound may be unreliable
selected_rules.execute: Use MRI to help clarify malignant potential.
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI is the most appropriate test to help clarify the malignant potential in patients where ultrasound may be unreliable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: extra-ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out
selected_rules.execute: Use CT imaging to assess for extra-ovarian disease.
selected_rules.source_statement: CT is most useful in cases where extra ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out.

selected_rules.activation_condition: using Doppler technology in adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Combine Doppler technology with morphological assessment.

selected_rules.execute: Use a morphological scoring system to differentiate benign from malignant masses.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and a recent office ultrasound was limited by overlying bowel gas, so the mass is not well characterized. She has no known prior ovarian disease. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify whether this isolated ovarian mass is benign or malignant. Because the initial ultrasound is technically unreliable, additional imaging is needed to better define malignant potential. There is no clear evidence yet of extra-ovarian spread, but the adnexal lesion remains suspicious and incompletely assessed.
recommended_actions: Use MRI to help clarify the malignant potential because ultrasound may be unreliable.
Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging to help differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette describes a suspicious isolated ovarian/adnexal mass with technically unreliable ultrasound, which triggers MRI as the most appropriate clarifying test. It also involves differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses, supporting use of sonography, MRI, or CT for characterization.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual uses indoor tanning devices

Avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at a younger age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cytogenetically normal AML

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows a normal karyotype, and there is no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
recommended_actions: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test in this patient with cytogenetically normal AML.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette describes newly diagnosed AML with cytogenetically normal disease, which directly activates the rule recommending DNMT3A mutation testing as part of biomarker evaluation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with newly diagnosed multiple myeloma being considered for initial transplant-based treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous SCT is the recommended treatment option for patients with newly diagnosed MM, as part of the initial treatment plan.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed MM patient is free from severe comorbidities and younger than 65 years after initial high-dose chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evidence included in four Clinical Practice Guidelines (CPGs) (1)(2)(3)(4) suggests that a single transplant with autologous SCT should be offered to all MM patients who are free from severe co-morbidities and who are younger than 65 years of age, following the initial treatment with high-dose chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient did not achieve complete remission after initial autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Offer a second autologous stem cell transplant.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evidence included in two CPGs (1,2) suggests that double autologous SCT should be offered to patients who did not achieve a complete remission after their initial autologous SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient relapses after a long remission following a single transplant
selected_rules.execute: Offer a second autologous transplant.
selected_rules.source_statement: ...the SCT Steering Committee's consensus opinion is that patients who relapse after a long remission following a single transplant should be offered a second transplant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed MM patient is age 65 or older but has good performance status and no comorbidities that contraindicate transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Consider offering autologous stem cell transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: The SCT Steering Committee acknowledged that, while the evidence upon which the CPGs were based almost uniformly excluded patients older than age 65, there was no reason not to offer SCT to patients 65 or older who have good performance status and no co-morbidities that would be a contraindication to transplantation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with MM responds to first autologous transplant with less than a very good partial response and has no progressive disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider tandem (double) autologous stem cell transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Tandem (double) autologous SCT is an option for patients with MM who respond to the first autologous transplant with less than a very good partial response, but not progressive disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has high-risk MM
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic transplantation, preferably within an investigative study.
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic transplantation is an option for patients with high-risk MM preferably within the context of an investigative study.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with high-risk MM is younger than 50 years and is not expected to benefit from autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation only in an investigative setting.
selected_rules.source_statement: ...it may be offered to patients <50 years of age who are not expected to benefit from autologous SCT (e.g., chromosome 13 deletion) within the investigative setting only (1,2,4).

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is newly diagnosed with symptomatic multiple myeloma after presenting with anemia, lytic bone lesions, and elevated serum protein. He has good functional status, walks daily, and has no major comorbidities. Baseline renal function and cardiac evaluation are acceptable, and he is being evaluated after induction therapy with high-dose chemotherapy for transplant-based treatment. He asks whether transplant should be part of his initial plan. He has not yet undergone any stem cell transplant and has no history of relapse or refractory disease.
recommended_actions: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant after initial high-dose chemotherapy, given that he is under 65 and free of severe comorbidities.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches a newly diagnosed adult with multiple myeloma being considered for initial transplant-based treatment, and he is also younger than 65 with no severe comorbidities after high-dose chemotherapy. That triggers the recommendations to include autologous stem cell transplantation in the initial plan and to offer a single autologous transplant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening is being offered outside a centre with experience and expertise in high-risk identification, interpretation, counselling, and diagnostic techniques
selected_rules.execute: Do not provide screening in that setting.
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group believes strongly that screening outside a centre with experience and expertise in identifying the high-risk population, interpreting results and counselling patients, and performing the appropriate diagnostic techniques is ill advised.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening is being offered in a setting without comprehensive care
selected_rules.execute: Do not offer screening in that setting.
selected_rules.source_statement: The ASCO guideline also supports screening of high-risk individuals, but only in the settings that can deliver comprehensive care such as that provided to NLST participants.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person is being screened based on other risk factors alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not expand eligibility solely on the basis of those other risk factors.
selected_rules.source_statement: there is currently insufficient evidence to include participants based on other risk factors such as family history, passive smoking, occupational exposure, radon exposure, previous cancer, and other diseases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person is high risk for lung cancer and eligible for screening
selected_rules.execute: Screen with low-dose CT (LDCT).
selected_rules.source_statement: Screening for lung cancer with LDCT is recommended in high-risk populations defined as persons 55 to 74 years of age with a minimum smoking history of ≥30 pack-years who currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years and are disease free at the time of screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person is 55 to 74 years old, has at least 30 pack-years smoking history, currently smokes or quit within past 15 years, and is disease free
selected_rules.execute: Offer LDCT screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: Screening for lung cancer with LDCT is recommended in high-risk populations defined as persons 55 to 74 years of age with a minimum smoking history of ≥30 pack-years who currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years and are disease free at the time of screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening program is being designed or delivered
selected_rules.execute: Use an organized, population-based screening program rather than ad hoc opportunistic screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: it is the opinion of the Working Group that screening should be conducted in a manner similar to the NLST trial: that is, in an organized fashion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening program is implemented
selected_rules.execute: Include smoking-cessation counseling in the program.
selected_rules.source_statement: The inclusion of smoking-cessation counseling within the screening program is crucial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening program is implemented
selected_rules.execute: Build in data collection, monitoring, quality assurance, and evaluation from the start.
selected_rules.source_statement: If elements of data collection and monitoring, quality assurance, and evaluation are built into the screening program from the start, it can be modified while in operation.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man presents to a community clinic asking for “a quick lung cancer scan” because his brother recently died of lung cancer. He has a 22 pack-year smoking history and quit 18 years ago. He feels well, has no respiratory symptoms, and has no known cancer diagnosis. The clinic is a small rural practice without a formal lung cancer screening pathway, specialist counseling support, or access to structured follow-up and diagnostic services. The patient asks whether he should be screened based on his family history and past smoking. The clinician is considering offering low-dose CT screening during the visit.
recommended_actions: Do not provide lung cancer screening in this setting because it lacks the experience and expertise needed for high-risk identification, interpretation, counselling, and diagnostic techniques.
Do not expand screening eligibility solely on the basis of family history or other non-smoking risk factors.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because screening is being offered outside a centre with the required expertise and diagnostic support. It also triggers R003 because the patient is requesting screening based on family history and past smoking, but he does not meet the evidence-based high-risk criteria; the rules state there is insufficient evidence to include people based on other risk factors alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Primary diagnosis of pancreatic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for primary diagnosis.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for primary diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Evaluating treatment response outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET to evaluate treatment response outside a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: The above results are based on two small nonrandomized studies and therefore are not strong enough to make a recommendation for using PET in evaluating treatment response outside of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Suspected recurrence of pancreatic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for clinical management of suspected recurrence.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for clinical management of suspected recurrence, nor for restaging at the time of recurrence, due to insufficient evidence and lack of effective therapeutic options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Restaging at the time of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for restaging at recurrence.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for clinical management of suspected recurrence, nor for restaging at the time of recurrence, due to insufficient evidence and lack of effective therapeutic options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Recurrent disease after surgical resection
selected_rules.execute: Restrict PET imaging to clinical trials.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET imaging in recurrent disease should be restricted to clinical trials.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is a candidate for potentially curative surgical resection based on conventional staging
selected_rules.execute: Perform PET for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging if a patient is a candidate for potentially curative surgical resection as determined by conventional staging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Suspicious pancreatic mass and standard investigations are not confirmatory
selected_rules.execute: Consider PET/CT to help establish the diagnosis.
selected_rules.source_statement: These pooled LRs suggest that PET and PET/CT offer small benefit in ruling in and ruling out pancreatic cancer when investigating a suspicious pancreatic mass; therefore, they may be useful in establishing a diagnosis when standard investigations are not confirmatory.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy is inconclusive or not possible and diagnosis remains in doubt
selected_rules.execute: Use PET/CT if a positive result would lead to surgical resection for diagnosis and treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: When biopsy is inconclusive or not possible and the diagnosis remains in doubt, the above evidence supports the use of PET/CT where a positive result would lead to surgical resection for purposes of both diagnosis and treatment.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with 3 months of epigastric pain, 8 kg unintentional weight loss, and new jaundice. Contrast CT shows a 2.8 cm mass in the pancreatic head with mild biliary dilation, but the lesion is still indeterminate on imaging. Endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy is attempted twice and is nondiagnostic due to scant tissue. He has no known metastatic disease, and the surgical team is considering whether he could be a candidate for potentially curative resection if the diagnosis is confirmed. The oncology team asks whether PET/CT should be used next to help clarify the diagnosis and guide management.
recommended_actions: Use PET/CT if a positive result would lead to surgical resection for diagnosis and treatment.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches suspected pancreatic cancer with inconclusive biopsy and persistent diagnostic uncertainty. Under these circumstances, PET/CT may be used if a positive result would change management by supporting surgical resection. No other rule is triggered because the case is not a confirmed primary diagnosis, not a recurrence, and not explicitly a preoperative staging scenario after conventional staging has already established resectability.

selected_rules.activation_condition: low rectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
selected_rules.source_statement: TRUS should not be used to predict CRM involvement.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering changing preoperative therapy selection criteria
selected_rules.execute: Do not change selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the mesorectal fascia, extramural depth of invasion, or extramural vascular invasion based on current evidence.
selected_rules.source_statement: To date, there is insufficient evidence to change the current selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the MRF (i.e., potential CRM), extramural depth of invasion (EMD) and/or extramural vascular invasion (EMVI).

selected_rules.activation_condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
selected_rules.execute: Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
selected_rules.source_statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
selected_rules.execute: Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the chest or chest X-ray.

selected_rules.activation_condition: CT chest is used for postoperative surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Do CT of the chest at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
selected_rules.source_statement: If CT of the chest is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest should be done at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance and chest X-ray shows abnormalities needing further workup
selected_rules.execute: Do CT of the chest.
selected_rules.source_statement: If chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest is recommended only if abnormalities requiring further investigation were found on chest X-ray.

selected_rules.activation_condition: CT chest is performed together with CT abdomen and pelvis
selected_rules.execute: Use intravenous contrast.
selected_rules.source_statement: When CT of the chest is performed in combination with CT of the abdomen and pelvis, intravenous contrast is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: preoperative complete colonic examination is possible
selected_rules.execute: Perform colonoscopy before surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: Complete colonic examination by colonoscopy should be performed preoperatively, if possible.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding and tenesmus. Colonoscopy shows a biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma in the low rectum, 5 cm from the anal verge. He is being evaluated for elective curative treatment and has no bowel obstruction, so a complete preoperative colonic examination is feasible. The surgical team is planning staging imaging before deciding on neoadjuvant therapy and resection strategy. The tumor is discussed as potentially close to the mesorectal fascia, but no prior imaging has yet been obtained. The patient asks whether transrectal ultrasound can determine whether the circumferential resection margin is involved.
recommended_actions: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis for staging.
Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
Perform colonoscopy before surgery, if complete colonic examination is possible.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette includes low rectal cancer, which triggers the recommendation against using TRUS to predict CRM involvement. Because he is undergoing elective treatment for rectal cancer, staging should include CT of the abdomen and pelvis and chest staging with CT of the chest or chest X-ray. Since a complete colonic examination is possible preoperatively, colonoscopy before surgery is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.
selected_rules.source_statement: At this time, induction CT before concurrent CT and RT should be considered an investigational approach.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal after chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Do not give maintenance chemotherapy after chemoradiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: James et al. 2013 (ACT II), studied maintenance chemotherapy versus none following chemoradiation and found that maintenance chemotherapy does not improve overall survival or colostomy-free survival. Therefore, maintenance chemotherapy following chemoradiation is not recommended in the management of squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and consideration of radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.execute: Avoid radiotherapy alone when concurrent chemoradiation is feasible.
selected_rules.source_statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal
selected_rules.execute: Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
selected_rules.source_statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal receiving concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Use 5-fluorouracil plus mitomycin C as the concurrent chemotherapy regimen with radiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: The optimal CT drug combination for squamous cell cancer of the anal canal is 5fluorouracil (5FU) plus mitomycin C (MMC), given concurrently with radiation treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal who is HIV-positive
selected_rules.execute: Manage the patient the same way as a patient without known HIV, while monitoring for potentially higher toxicity.
selected_rules.source_statement: It is the expert opinion of the Gastrointestinal Cancer Disease Site Group (GI DSG) that HIV-positive patients with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal should be managed in the same way as patients without known HIV. Treating physicians should be aware that a greater than average risk of toxicity is possible.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and need to choose a mitomycin C schedule
selected_rules.execute: No preferred mitomycin C dosing schedule can be recommended from the available data.
selected_rules.source_statement: In the trials using MMC in the 5FU-MMC combination regimens, MMC schedules include dose of 12 or 15mg/me day 1 only, and a 10mg/m2 Day 1, 29 dosing. There is no comparative data to allow a recommendation of a preferred schedule.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal presents for treatment planning. Staging CT shows no distant metastases, and he is otherwise fit with good renal and marrow function. He asks whether he can start with a short course of chemotherapy before radiation to shrink the tumor first. The oncology team is also discussing whether radiation alone might be simpler, but he is able to tolerate combined treatment. He is HIV-negative and has no major comorbidities. The team plans definitive therapy in the coming weeks and is deciding on the concurrent regimen.
recommended_actions: Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.
generated_case.rationale: The patient has localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiation, which triggers the recommendation against induction therapy as standard care. Because he is biopsy-proven localized disease and can tolerate combined treatment, concurrent chemoradiation is recommended over radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all institutions administering hazardous drugs
selected_rules.execute: form a committee responsible for policies and procedures for hazardous drugs
selected_rules.source_statement: It is strongly recommended that all institutions administering hazardous drugs form such a committee.

selected_rules.activation_condition: committee for hazardous drugs is formed
selected_rules.execute: include representatives from occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology, environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative
selected_rules.source_statement: It is also strongly recommended that this committee include, but not be limited to, representatives from various departments and services such as: occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology (physician), environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug policies and procedures are being developed or revised
selected_rules.execute: use clear processes for developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures
selected_rules.source_statement: This committee would be responsible for clear processes of developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures related to hazardous drugs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug program oversight is in place
selected_rules.execute: routinely conduct risk assessment and gap analysis
selected_rules.source_statement: A risk assessment and gap analysis should be routinely conducted to identify gaps and to inform policies and procedures.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug staff orientation and education are needed
selected_rules.execute: ensure a process for orientation and ongoing education for the target population
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, this committee is responsible for ensuring that there is a process in place for orientation and ongoing education for the identified target population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug risk prevention management program is in place
selected_rules.execute: implement and follow up the Risk Prevention Management Program
selected_rules.source_statement: This committee is responsible for implementation and follow-up of the Risk Prevention Management Program related to the use of hazardous drugs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: staff are involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit
selected_rules.execute: provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education including safe handling and spill or leak management
selected_rules.source_statement: It is legislated that initial and ongoing hospital-approved education be provided to all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit including safe handling and spill or leak management (6).

selected_rules.activation_condition: staff work with hazardous drugs
selected_rules.execute: provide initial and ongoing training on best practice standards
selected_rules.source_statement: It is strongly recommended that all staff have initial and ongoing training related to best practice standards in place at the time.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to an oncology unit for her next cycle of chemotherapy. The unit recently expanded outpatient infusion services, and the pharmacy director notes that staff from pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services all handle hazardous drugs at different points in the medication circuit. During a quality review, several nurses report that spill response steps are not consistently taught during onboarding, and new float staff are uncertain about safe handling procedures. The oncology service is planning to update its hazardous-drug workflow and wants to reduce variation in practice across shifts. No medication error has occurred, but the team recognizes that the current process depends heavily on informal teaching and individual experience.
recommended_actions: Provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education for all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit, including safe handling and spill or leak management.
Provide initial and ongoing training for all staff working with hazardous drugs on current best-practice standards.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette involves staff handling hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit and identifies gaps in onboarding and spill management teaching, which triggers the requirement for hospital-approved education. It also describes staff working with hazardous drugs and needing standardized preparation, which triggers ongoing training on best-practice standards.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to determine diagnosis or grading
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for diagnosis or grading in gliomas.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the determination of diagnosis or grading in gliomas.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to add diagnostic accuracy beyond MRI and histology
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET to improve diagnostic accuracy or prognostic assessment beyond MRI and histology.
selected_rules.source_statement: none have demonstrated any additional diagnostic accuracy or prognostic influence over and above that provided by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and histology in a multivariate model.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with glioblastoma multiforme has suspected radiation necrosis versus tumour recurrence and PET/CT is being considered
selected_rules.execute: PET/CT may be considered as anecdotal evidence suggests it may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, recognizing the lack of a gold standard.
selected_rules.source_statement: Anecdotal evidence exists that PET/CT may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, but there is no gold standard for the diagnosis of radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with glioma is being assessed for treatment response using PET
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made for or against PET for treatment response assessment.
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and PET or PET/CT is being considered for assessment
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made for or against PET or PET/CT in recurrent gliomas.
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET or PET/CT in the assessment of patients with recurrent gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and FDG PET is being considered
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made for FDG PET in recurrent gliomas because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence was insufficient to generate a recommendation on the use of FDG PET.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being evaluated and PET/CT before and after treatment is proposed to correlate with survival
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume evidence supports prospective PET/CT assessment before and after treatment for survival correlation; evidence is lacking.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET or PET/CT has not been examined in a prospective cohort of gliomas to assess the treatment effect on PET imaging before and after treatment and correlate this with survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being assessed and radiation necrosis is a major diagnostic issue
selected_rules.execute: Recognize radiation necrosis as a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas; no specific PET recommendation is provided.
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiation necrosis is a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a prior diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme completed chemoradiation 10 months ago. He now reports worsening headaches and mild word-finding difficulty. MRI shows a new enhancing lesion near the prior resection cavity, but the findings are equivocal for either tumor recurrence or radiation necrosis. His oncology team is considering PET/CT to help clarify the diagnosis before changing therapy. He has no fever, seizures, or other signs of infection, and his neurologic exam is otherwise stable. The main question is whether the new imaging abnormality represents recurrent tumor or treatment-related necrosis.
recommended_actions: PET/CT may be considered because anecdotal evidence suggests it may help differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence in glioblastoma multiforme.
Interpret any PET/CT result cautiously, recognizing that there is no gold standard for diagnosing radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches glioblastoma multiforme with suspected radiation necrosis versus tumour recurrence, which triggers the conditional consideration for PET/CT. The recommendation is limited to cautious consideration because the supporting evidence is anecdotal and there is no gold standard for radiation necrosis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient has not had mutation testing or is only clinically selected by ethnicity, sex, histology, or smoking status
selected_rules.execute: Do not use first-line EGFR TKI therapy based on clinical selection alone
selected_rules.source_statement: First-line therapy with an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) is not recommended in unselected (patients who have not undergone mutation testing) or clinically selected populations of patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient is being considered for first-line EGFR TKI based on Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology, or light/never smoking status
selected_rules.execute: Do not use these clinical characteristics to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The use of clinical characteristics such as Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology and light/never smoking status is not recommended to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy, as this strategy does not reliably select patients who have mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has progressed after chemotherapy and previously received gefitinib or erlotinib
selected_rules.execute: Do not use a second EGFR TKI such as afatinib because evidence is insufficient and no overall survival improvement is shown
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend the use of a second EGFR TKI, such as afatinib, in patients whose disease has progressed following chemotherapy and gefitinib or erlotinib, as available data does not demonstrate any improvement in overall survival

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is receiving high-dose gefitinib rather than standard-dose gefitinib
selected_rules.execute: Avoid higher-dose gefitinib because grade 3 or 4 toxicity is higher and ILD-type events occurred only with 500 mg/day in one trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Two randomized phase II trials ... identified that grade 3 or 4 toxicity was higher with the higher dose gefitinib. Interstitial lung disease-type events occurred in only one of the two trials, and only with 500 mg/day gefitinib.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease
selected_rules.execute: Use first-line EGFR TKI therapy instead of platinum-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with EGFR mutation-positive NSCLC, first-line therapy with an EGFR TKI such as gefitinib, erlotinib or afatinib is the preferred treatment compared to platinum-based therapies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has progressed after platinum-based chemotherapy and is well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use an EGFR TKI as second- or third-line therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy, an EGFR TKI can be recommended as second-or third-line therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for maintenance therapy after four cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy and has not progressed
selected_rules.execute: Use an EGFR TKI as maintenance therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: An EGFR TKI is recommended as an option for maintenance therapy in patients who have not progressed after four cycles of a platinum-doublet chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease and an EGFR TKI is being chosen
selected_rules.execute: Choose among gefitinib, erlotinib, or afatinib based on expected toxicity and cost because no one agent is supported over another
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no evidence to support one EGFR TKI over another, so the decision about which EGFR TKI to use should take into consideration the expected toxicity of the drug as well as the cost.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with newly diagnosed metastatic non-small cell lung cancer is referred after biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. She is a never-smoker and had been presumed to be a good candidate for an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor based on her sex, histology, and smoking history. However, her molecular testing has not yet been performed, and no EGFR mutation status is available. Her oncologist is considering starting gefitinib immediately because of her clinical profile, before mutation results return. She is otherwise stable, with no major organ dysfunction and no prior systemic therapy.
recommended_actions: Do not use first-line EGFR TKI therapy based on clinical selection alone.
Do not use Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology, or light/never smoking status to select the patient for first-line EGFR TKI therapy.
Obtain mutation testing before choosing EGFR TKI therapy.
generated_case.rationale: The patient has NSCLC without EGFR mutation testing results, and the proposed EGFR TKI would be chosen solely on clinical characteristics. This matches rules against using unselected or clinically selected populations for first-line EGFR TKI therapy. Because mutation status is unknown, the correct action is to avoid selecting therapy on clinical features alone and to wait for molecular testing.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient phenotype matches the known spectrum for the gene
selected_rules.execute: Do not use phenotype match alone as evidence for pathogenicity.
selected_rules.source_statement: In general, the fact that a patient has a phenotype that matches the known spectrum of clinical features for a gene is not considered evidence for pathogenicity given that nearly all patients undergoing diseasetargeted tests have the phenotype in question.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Applying variant classification rules to a GUS
selected_rules.execute: Do not use variant classification rules developed for recognized genotype-phenotype associations.
selected_rules.source_statement: Special care must be taken when applying the recommended guidelines to a GUS. In such situations, utilizing variant classification rules developed for recognized genotype-phenotype associations is not appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering whether to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline independently
selected_rules.execute: Do not invest extensive effort to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline when endorsement is sufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: Further, the Working Group felt that investing extensive effort to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline would not be justified given the number of experts involved in its creation.
