generated_case.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old woman is referred for interpretation of a heterozygous missense variant found on a hereditary cardiomyopathy panel after she developed exertional dyspnea and mild left ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiogram. The laboratory notes that her clinical picture fits the known spectrum for the gene, but the variant is also listed in a public database with several submissions and a relatively high allele frequency in a general population dataset. The report does not clearly state the genome build, transcript used, or whether the database entries were curated or independently observed. The cardiologist asks whether the phenotype alone is enough to support pathogenicity and whether the public database evidence is sufficient to classify the variant.
recommended_actions: Do not use the phenotype match alone as evidence for pathogenicity.
When using public databases, check how often the database is updated, whether curation is supported, and what curation methods were used.
Confirm Human Genome Variation Society nomenclature and identify the genome build and transcript references used for naming the variant.
Determine the source of the observations and whether the observations are independent.
Because the allele frequency in a control population is greater than expected for a rare Mendelian disorder, use this as strong support for a benign interpretation (BS1).
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers rules about phenotype matching, public database evaluation, and allele frequency. The phenotype is compatible with the gene’s spectrum, but that alone should not be treated as evidence for pathogenicity. The database details are incomplete, so the relevant metadata and data quality should be checked before relying on it. The elevated population frequency supports a benign interpretation under BS1.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and is a potential candidate for curative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use PET to improve the accuracy of M staging
selected_rules.source_statement: For the staging workup of patients with esophageal cancer who are potential candidates for curative therapy, PET is recommended to improve the accuracy of M staging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and staging workup is being done
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for its incremental value in detecting distant disease in addition to CT +/- endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)
selected_rules.source_statement: There is a significant role for PET for its incremental value in detecting distant disease, in addition to CT +/-endoscopic ultrasound (EUS).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and EUS is not available
selected_rules.execute: Use PET because it provides incremental benefit when EUS is absent
selected_rules.source_statement: Especially in the absence of EUS, PET provides an incremental benefit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has newly diagnosed esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider PET/CT for nodal staging because it may be superior to PET alone
selected_rules.source_statement: There is some evidence that PET/CT is superior to PET alone for nodal staging (Yuan et al, 2006.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has newly diagnosed esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use PET or PET/CT to improve clinical management, prognostic stratification, prediction of regional and locoregional lymph nodes, and pretreatment staging accuracy compared with CT and EUS alone
selected_rules.source_statement: also showed the significant impact of PET and PET/CT on the clinical management, prognostic stratification of patients with newly diagnosed esophageal cancer, prediction of regional and locoregional lymph nodes, and improvement on the accuracy of pretreatment staging compared to CT and EUS alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: PET is used early in treatment or at completion of neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use PET as a possible predictor of complete pathologic response and longer-term survival or event-free survival
selected_rules.source_statement: There is some evidence that PET, either early in treatment or at the completion of neoadjuvant therapy, can predict complete pathologic response, and therefore, predict the longer-term outcome in terms of survival and event-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Assessing response after neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider PET as it may be superior to CT and comparable to EUS for assessment of response and prognosis
selected_rules.source_statement: One systematic review of four primary studies plus one additional study showed that PET may be superior to CT and comparable to EUS in the assessment of response and of prognosis after neoadjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Assessing response after neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider PET/CT because it may be more sensitive than CT or endoscopic ultrasound for response evaluation
selected_rules.source_statement: One additional study showed PET/CT to be more sensitive for the evaluation of response than either CT or endoscopic ultrasound.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia and a 10-kg unintentional weight loss is newly diagnosed with distal esophageal adenocarcinoma on biopsy. CT chest/abdomen shows a circumferential distal esophageal mass with a few borderline paraesophageal nodes but no definite distant metastases. He is otherwise fit, has good functional status, and the oncology team is considering curative-intent chemoradiation followed by surgery if staging confirms localized disease. Endoscopic ultrasound is not available at the referring center, so the team wants the most accurate preoperative staging possible before proceeding.
recommended_actions: Use PET to improve the accuracy of M staging.
Use PET for its incremental value in detecting distant disease in addition to CT +/- endoscopic ultrasound (EUS).
Use PET because it provides incremental benefit when EUS is absent.
Consider PET/CT for nodal staging because it may be superior to PET alone.
Use PET or PET/CT to improve clinical management, prognostic stratification, prediction of regional and locoregional lymph nodes, and pretreatment staging accuracy compared with CT and EUS alone.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes newly diagnosed esophageal cancer in a patient who is a potential candidate for curative therapy and is undergoing staging workup, with EUS unavailable. Those features trigger the preoperative staging rules supporting PET, including its incremental value for distant disease detection and improved M staging accuracy. Because the cancer is newly diagnosed, the rules also support considering PET/CT for nodal staging and using PET or PET/CT to improve pretreatment staging and clinical management compared with CT and EUS alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease
selected_rules.execute: do not order routine biomarker or blood tests for screening
selected_rules.source_statement: 3.2 Routine biomarker or blood tests to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease are not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IA, IB, or IIA melanoma and is clinically disease-free after curative-intent treatment
selected_rules.execute: perform clinical follow-up with history and physical examination including full skin and lymph node examination every 6 to 12 months for 3 years, then annually for 2 years or as clinically indicated; dermatologist, surgeon, family physician, or cancer nurse specialist may provide follow-up; photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with stage IA, IB, or IIA melanoma who are clinically disease-free after receiving curative-intent treatment: 1.1 Clinical follow-up with history and physical examination with full skin and lymph node examination by a dermatologist (with photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated), and/or a surgeon, family physician, cancer nurse specialists should occur every six to 12 months for three years, then annually for two years or as clinically indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma
selected_rules.execute: perform clinical follow-up with history and physical examination including full skin and lymph node examination every 3 to 6 months in years 1 to 3, then every 6 months in years 4 to 5, or as clinically indicated; dermatologist, surgeon, medical oncologist, or cancer nurse specialist may provide follow-up; photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma: 3.1 Clinical follow-up with history and physical examination with full skin and lymph node examination by a dermatologist (with photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated), and/or a surgeon, medical oncologist, or cancer nurse specialist should occur every three to six months in years 1 to 3, then every six months in years 4 to 5, or as clinically indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: brain MRI is being used for routine screening and is available
selected_rules.execute: prefer MRI of the brain without radiation
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI (no radiation) of the brain is preferred for routine screening where available, otherwise, head CT may be considered after discussing with patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has a positive sentinel lymph node and no complete lymph node dissection was performed
selected_rules.execute: perform ultrasound scans of the draining nodal basin every 4 to 6 months for years 1 to 3, then every 6 months for years 4 to 5
selected_rules.source_statement: 3.5 For patients with a positive sentinel lymph node, ultrasound scans of the draining nodal basin should be done every four to six months for years 1 to 3, and then every six months for years 4 to 5, if no complete lymph node dissection is performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3
selected_rules.execute: consider CT or PET/CT scans every 6 to 12 months
selected_rules.source_statement: 3.3 CT or PET/CT scans every six to 12 months should be considered to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3, then annually in years 4 to 5.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 4 to 5
selected_rules.execute: consider CT or PET/CT scans annually
selected_rules.source_statement: 3.3 CT or PET/CT scans every six to 12 months should be considered to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3, then annually in years 4 to 5.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma
selected_rules.execute: consider annual brain MRI for years 1 to 5
selected_rules.source_statement: 3.4 Annual brain MRI can be considered for years 1 to 5.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents for routine oncology follow-up after resection of a stage IIIC cutaneous melanoma from the upper back 14 months ago. He completed sentinel node biopsy showing one positive node, and no completion lymph node dissection was performed. He feels well and denies weight loss, headaches, cough, bone pain, new skin lesions, or neurologic symptoms. Physical examination shows a well-healed scar, no palpable lymphadenopathy, and no concerning skin findings. He asks whether he should have blood tests to look for recurrence, since he is worried about metastatic disease. He is otherwise healthy and remains asymptomatic.
recommended_actions: Do not order routine biomarker or blood tests to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease.

Continue clinical follow-up with history and physical examination, including full skin and lymph node examination, every 3 to 6 months in years 1 to 3, then every 6 months in years 4 to 5, or as clinically indicated.

Perform ultrasound scans of the draining nodal basin every 4 to 6 months for years 1 to 3, then every 6 months for years 4 to 5, because he had a positive sentinel lymph node and no complete lymph node dissection was performed.
generated_case.rationale: This patient has resected stage IIIC melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence, which triggers the recommendation against routine biomarker or blood tests. His stage also triggers standard clinical follow-up every 3 to 6 months in years 1 to 3, then every 6 months in years 4 to 5. Because he had a positive sentinel lymph node without completion lymph node dissection, draining nodal basin ultrasound surveillance is also recommended on the specified schedule.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with endometrial cancer after potentially curative primary treatment and no evidence of disease
selected_rules.execute: counsel about potential symptoms of recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that all patients receive counselling about the potential symptoms of recurrence of endometrial cancer, because the majority of recurrences in the identified studies were symptomatic.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is surgically or pathologically confirmed low risk of recurrence (stage IA or IB, grade 1 or 2)
selected_rules.execute: perform general examination including complete history and pelvic-rectal examination semi-annually or annually for the first 3 years, then annually for the next 2 years
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients at a surgically or pathologically confirmed low risk of recurrence (i.e., stage IA or IB, grade 1 or 2): A general examination, including a complete history and a pelvicrectal examination, conducted semi-annually or annually for the first three years and annually for the next two years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is high risk of recurrence (stage IA or IB, grade 3, or stage IC or advanced stage)
selected_rules.execute: perform general examination including complete history and pelvic-rectal examination every 3 to 6 months for the first 3 years, then semi-annually for the next 2 years
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients at high risk of recurrence (i.e., stage IA or IB, grade 3, or stage IC or advanced stage). A general examination, including a complete history and a pelvic-rectal examination, every three to six months for the first three years and semi-annually for the next two years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: planning follow-up setting or specialist type for any patient
selected_rules.execute: follow the patient with a health care professional knowledgeable about the natural history of the disease and comfortable performing speculum and pelvic exams
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to inform the optimum clinical setting or type of specialist required for follow-up; however, it is recommended that all patients be followed by a health care professional who is knowledgeable about the natural history of the disease, and who is comfortable performing speculum and pelvic exams, in order to diagnose or detect a local (vaginal) recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has symptoms suggestive of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: perform targeted investigation to rule out recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that all patients undergo a targeted investigation to rule out recurrence if symptomatic, since patients with local recurrence are potentially curable with further therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: treatment includes radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: counsel the patient on potential adverse effects of radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Where treatment with radiotherapy is involved, it is recommended that patients be counselled on the potential adverse effects of radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with endometrial cancer after potentially curative primary treatment and no evidence of disease
selected_rules.execute: use a follow-up strategy based on recurrence risk and patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: The most appropriate follow-up strategy is likely one based upon the risk of recurrence, with individual patient preferences for more or less follow-up taken into account.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has remained recurrence-free for 5 years
selected_rules.execute: return to annual population-based general physical and pelvic examination
selected_rules.source_statement: Since the majority of patients with recurrence were symptomatic and virtually all recurred within five years, it seems reasonable that patients return to annual population-based general physical and pelvic examination after five years of recurrence-free follow-up.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman is seen in oncology follow-up 8 months after undergoing total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for stage IB, grade 2 endometrioid endometrial cancer. Final pathology showed no residual disease outside the uterus, and she has no current evidence of disease. She reports feeling well overall and denies vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, new cough, weight loss, or bowel or bladder changes. She completed treatment without radiotherapy. She asks what follow-up should include and who should see her. She is anxious about recurrence and wants to know which symptoms should prompt an earlier visit.
recommended_actions: Counsel the patient about potential symptoms of recurrence of endometrial cancer.

Arrange general examination, including complete history and pelvic-rectal examination, semi-annually or annually for the first 3 years, then annually for the next 2 years, because she is low risk of recurrence.
Follow her with a health care professional knowledgeable about the natural history of the disease and comfortable performing speculum and pelvic exams.
Use a follow-up strategy based on recurrence risk and the patient’s preferences.
generated_case.rationale: This patient has endometrial cancer after potentially curative primary treatment with no evidence of disease, so counselling about symptoms of recurrence is recommended. Her surgically/pathologically confirmed stage IB, grade 2 disease places her in the low-risk group, supporting semi-annual or annual general examination with history and pelvic-rectal exam for the first 3 years, then annually for 2 more years. Follow-up should be with a clinician comfortable with speculum and pelvic exams, and the overall strategy should consider recurrence risk and patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: general population at average or low risk
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely perform total-body skin examination for screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the limited evidence available at present, routine total body skin examination by primary care providers is not recommended for individuals at average or low risk for skin cancer (i.e., those not included in the increased risk groups described above).

selected_rules.activation_condition: general population at average or low risk
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely counsel on skin self-examination for screening.

selected_rules.execute: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.

selected_rules.activation_condition: very high risk due to more than 100 nevi or 5 or more atypical nevi
selected_rules.execute: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.

selected_rules.activation_condition: very high risk due to more than 250 PUVA treatments for psoriasis
selected_rules.execute: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.
selected_rules.source_statement: Individuals at very high risk should be identified by their primary health care provider and offered total body skin examination by a dermatologist or a trained health care provider on a yearly basis.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a full skin check. She has fair skin, no personal history of skin cancer, no organ transplant history, no chronic immunosuppressive therapy, and no family history of melanoma. She reports about 20 common nevi, none atypical, and no prior PUVA treatment. She has not noticed any changing or symptomatic skin lesions. Her main concern is prevention because a friend recently had a melanoma diagnosis. She is otherwise healthy and at average risk for skin cancer based on the history provided.
recommended_actions: Do not routinely perform total-body skin examination for screening.
Do not routinely counsel on skin self-examination for screening.
generated_case.rationale: The patient fits the general average/low-risk population and does not meet any very high-risk criteria. Therefore, the rules support not routinely offering total-body skin examination screening and not routinely counseling on skin self-examination for screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with neuroendocrine cancer who is inoperable, has residual disease after surgery or other ablative therapy, or has metastases
selected_rules.execute: Consider PRRT as an acceptable treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: PRRT appears to be an acceptable option in adult patients with neuroendocrine cancer who are inoperable, have residual disease following surgery or other ablative therapy, or have metastases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using PRRT, especially 90 Y-DOTALAN or 177 Lu-DOTATATE
selected_rules.execute: Use renal protection with lysine and arginine amino acid solution and monitor renal function
selected_rules.source_statement: PRRT is relatively safe and well tolerated with renal protection using lysine and arginine amino acid solution, especially for 90 Y-DOTALAN and 177 Lu-DOTATATE. However, renal function must be monitored.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Providing PRRT in Ontario
selected_rules.execute: Conduct PRRT as part of one or more RCTs, or large comparative clinical trials if an RCT is not feasible, under a Clinical Trials Agreement
selected_rules.source_statement: Treatment with PRRT in Ontario should be conducted as part of one or more RCTs, or in large comparative clinical trials if an RCT is not feasible, under the authority of a Clinical Trials Agreement, to clarify the further effects of PRRT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering 177 Lu-DOTATATE for future PRRT trials
selected_rules.execute: Include 177 Lu-DOTATATE as an appropriate agent in future clinical trials
selected_rules.source_statement: Therefore, 177 Lu-DOTATATE would be an appropriate agent to include in the future clinical trials described above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is to receive PRRT or 131 I-MIBG
selected_rules.execute: Confirm that the tumour demonstrates positive uptake of the related diagnostic agent before therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Prior to the administration of therapy, the tumours from NET patients who are to receive PRRT or 131 I-MIBG should demonstrate a positive uptake of the related diagnostic agent.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using 131 I-MIBG
selected_rules.execute: Consider hematologic toxicity, severe infections, and secondary malignancies in decision-making
selected_rules.source_statement: However, the hematologic toxicity, severe infections, and secondary malignancies possible afterwards should be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Tumour uptake on octreoscan is higher than or equal to liver uptake before PRRT
selected_rules.execute: Proceed with PRRT only when uptake is positive on diagnostic imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: All the patient tumours showed a higher or the same uptake on octreoscan than on liver uptake before PRRT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using 90 Y-DOTATOC, 90 Y-DOTATATE, or 177 Lu-DOTATATE

selected_rules.source_statement: For studies investigating the effects of 90 Y-DOTATOC, 90 Y-DOTATATE, and 177 Lu-DOTATATE, lysine and arginine amino acid solution was infused to protect kidney function.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with metastatic well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of the ileum presents after prior debulking surgery. He remains symptomatic with flushing and diarrhea, and imaging shows persistent liver metastases that are not resectable. His somatostatin receptor scan demonstrates uptake in the lesions that is higher than liver uptake. He has preserved baseline renal function and is being evaluated for peptide receptor radionuclide therapy with 177 Lu-DOTATATE. The oncology team discusses treatment options and plans therapy if eligibility is confirmed.
recommended_actions: Consider PRRT as an acceptable treatment option.
Confirm that the tumour demonstrates positive uptake of the related diagnostic agent before therapy.
Proceed with PRRT only when uptake is positive on diagnostic imaging.
Infuse lysine and arginine amino acid solution to protect kidney function.
generated_case.rationale: The patient is an adult with metastatic neuroendocrine cancer and unresectable residual disease, matching the PRRT eligibility rule. His diagnostic scan is positive with uptake at least as high as liver, supporting PRRT readiness. Because 177 Lu-DOTATATE is being considered, renal-protective amino acid infusion is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving alemtuzumab consolidation after response to first-line fludarabine-containing chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Avoid routine consolidation with alemtuzumab because the trial was stopped early for severe infections
selected_rules.source_statement: The trial was stopped after the accrual of 21 patients due to the occurrence of the National Cancer Institute Common Toxicity Criteria (NCI-CTC) Version 2.0 grade III/IV infections in seven of the first 11 patients randomized to alemtuzumab consolidation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with progressive and symptomatic CLL refractory to both alkylator-based and fludarabine-based regimens
selected_rules.execute: Consider alemtuzumab as a reasonable treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Treatment with alemtuzumab is a reasonable option for patients with progressive and symptomatic CLL that is refractory to both alkylator-based and fludarabine-based regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient was previously treated with alkylating agents and is refractory to fludarabine without bulky lymphadenopathy
selected_rules.execute: Consider alemtuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: the UK CLL Forum guideline recommended alemtuzumab for use in patients without bulky lymphadenopathy (<5cm), who were previously treated with alkylating agents and refractory to fludarabine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering alemtuzumab for CLL
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient that evidence for benefit on duration of response, quality of life, and overall survival is not established and remains speculative
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be informed that any possible beneficial effect of alemtuzumab on other outcome measures such as duration of response, quality of life, and overall survival are not supported in evidence and remain speculative at this time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering alemtuzumab for CLL
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient that treatment has significant and potentially serious toxicities and that the balance of harm versus benefit is uncertain
selected_rules.source_statement: Treatment with alemtuzumab is associated with significant and potentially serious adverse treatment-related toxicities. Patients must be carefully informed of the uncertain balance between potential risks of harm and the chance for benefit reported in studies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering alemtuzumab for CLL
selected_rules.execute: Use patient preferences as an important factor in deciding whether to treat
selected_rules.source_statement: Given the current substantial uncertainty in this balance, patient preferences will likely play a large role in determining the appropriate treatment choice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has important co-morbidities
selected_rules.execute: Use alemtuzumab cautiously because excessive risk may be present
selected_rules.source_statement: Given the potential toxicities associated with alemtuzumab, and given the limited nature of the clinical trials testing its use in broad populations of patients with CLL, the use of alemtuzumab in patients with important co-morbidities may be associated with excessive risks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has relapsed or chemotherapy-refractory CLL
selected_rules.execute: Recognize alemtuzumab as an active agent that can produce responses, but note that complete remissions are uncommon
selected_rules.source_statement: The DSG regards alemtuzumab as an active agent for the treatment of patients with relapsed or chemotherapy-refractory CLL. This conclusion is based on response data from single-arm studies that report partial responses in approximately one third of patients and recognizes that complete remissions are uncommon.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia presents with worsening fatigue, night sweats, and enlarging cervical nodes. He previously received an alkylating-agent regimen and later fludarabine-based therapy, but his disease has now relapsed and is progressing despite treatment. He has no bulky lymphadenopathy, with the largest node measuring 3 cm on exam. His blood counts show mild anemia and lymphocytosis, and he is asking about next-line options. He is otherwise functional but has a history of stable coronary artery disease and diabetes. The oncologist is considering alemtuzumab and discusses the potential benefits and risks with him.
recommended_actions: Consider alemtuzumab because he previously received alkylating agents and is refractory to fludarabine without bulky lymphadenopathy.
Inform the patient that evidence for benefit on duration of response, quality of life, and overall survival is not established and remains speculative.
Inform the patient that treatment has significant and potentially serious toxicities and that the balance of harm versus benefit is uncertain.
Use patient preferences as an important factor in deciding whether to treat.
Use alemtuzumab cautiously because important co-morbidities may increase risk.
Recognize alemtuzumab as an active agent that can produce responses, but note that complete remissions are uncommon.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette fits relapsed, chemotherapy-refractory CLL after alkylator and fludarabine exposure without bulky lymphadenopathy, triggering the recommendation to consider alemtuzumab. Because alemtuzumab is being considered, the patient should be counseled that evidence for longer-term outcomes is speculative, that serious toxicities and uncertain risk-benefit balance are important, and that patient preferences should guide the decision. His coronary artery disease and diabetes support cautious use due to comorbidity-related risk. The response data also justify noting that alemtuzumab can work, but complete remissions are uncommon.

selected_rules.source_statement: A person should be referred if presenting with any of the following: - Superior vena cava syndrome/obstruction

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has signs or symptoms suspicious of lung cancer
selected_rules.execute: order a chest X-ray within two working days
selected_rules.source_statement: Indications for Chest X-ray A person should have a chest X-ray within two working days if they present with any of the following:

selected_rules.activation_condition: chest X-ray is suspicious for lung cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure the report clearly notes suspicion of lung cancer and use direct notification methods to inform the family physician or other primary care provider
selected_rules.source_statement: If the chest Xray is suspicious for lung cancer, this must be clearly noted on the X-ray report. Radiologists should consider using two or more mechanisms to directly inform the FP or other PCPs of the suspicion of lung cancer. (e.g., fax, flagging, telephone call, email)

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents to his family doctor with 3 weeks of cough, intermittent blood-streaked sputum, and mild unintentional weight loss. He denies fever or recent infection. His oxygen saturation is normal, but he reports the hemoptysis has occurred on several mornings over the past week. There is no stridor, no focal neurological deficit, and no symptoms of superior vena cava obstruction. The clinician is concerned about possible lung cancer and orders an urgent chest X-ray the same day.
recommended_actions: Arrange urgent chest CT and/or urgent referral to a diagnostic assessment program or thoracic surgeon because he has persistent non-massive hemoptysis.
Order a chest X-ray within two working days because he has signs and symptoms suspicious of lung cancer.
Do not use sputum cytology for the investigation of suspected lung cancer.
generated_case.rationale: Persistent non-massive hemoptysis triggers urgent referral/CT, and the overall presentation is suspicious for lung cancer, which warrants prompt chest X-ray. Because suspected lung cancer is being investigated, sputum cytology is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has cardiac dysfunction and perioperative chemotherapy with ECF is being considered
selected_rules.execute: do not use epirubicin
selected_rules.source_statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has renal dysfunction and perioperative chemotherapy with ECF is being considered
selected_rules.execute: do not use cisplatin
selected_rules.source_statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for perioperative chemotherapy and has cardiac dysfunction
selected_rules.execute: avoid epirubicin-containing perioperative chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for perioperative chemotherapy and has renal dysfunction
selected_rules.execute: avoid cisplatin-containing perioperative chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has resectable gastric cancer and is being considered for adjunct treatment after curative surgery
selected_rules.execute: consider postoperative chemoradiation using the Macdonald protocol or perioperative ECF using the MAGIC protocol
selected_rules.source_statement: OS in patients with resectable gastric cancer is significantly improved with the use of either postoperative chemoradiation implementing the Macdonald protocol (47-49) or perioperative ECF implementing the MAGIC protocol (30).

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with resectable gastric cancer
selected_rules.execute: perform a multidisciplinary assessment to determine the best plan of care
selected_rules.source_statement: All patients with resectable gastric cancer should undergo a multidisciplinary assessment to determine the best plan of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is undergoing gastric surgery with curative intent and there is no obvious contraindication due to poor performance status
selected_rules.execute: consider adjuncts to resection
selected_rules.source_statement: Unless obviously contraindicated owing to poor performance status, all patients undergoing gastric surgery with curative intent should be considered for adjuncts to resection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: planning postoperative chemoradiation target volume
selected_rules.execute: target the upper abdomen including the tumour bed and regional nodes, extending 2 cm beyond the proximal and distal margin of resection
selected_rules.source_statement: The target volume is in the upper abdomen targeting the tumour bed and regional nodes, 2 cm beyond the proximal and distal margin of resection.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with resected distal gastric adenocarcinoma returns after curative subtotal gastrectomy. Final pathology shows pT3N1 disease with negative margins, and he is recovering well enough to discuss adjuvant treatment. His case is reviewed in a multidisciplinary tumor board. He has a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35%, chronic exertional dyspnea, and prior hospitalization for decompensated heart failure. Renal function is preserved. The oncology team is considering perioperative chemotherapy options, including an ECF-based approach, but is concerned about his cardiac status. He has no major postoperative complications and is eager to pursue additional therapy if safe.
recommended_actions: Do not use epirubicin as part of perioperative ECF because the patient has cardiac dysfunction.
Avoid epirubicin-containing perioperative chemotherapy.
Consider other postoperative options for resectable gastric cancer, such as postoperative chemoradiation using the Macdonald protocol, rather than ECF.
Perform a multidisciplinary assessment to determine the best plan of care.
Consider adjuncts to resection since he is undergoing curative-intent gastric cancer surgery and does not have an obvious contraindication from poor performance status.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers rules for resectable gastric cancer after curative surgery, multidisciplinary review, and consideration of adjunct therapy. It also triggers the cardiac dysfunction contraindication to epirubicin-containing perioperative chemotherapy, so ECF should not be used. The recommended actions are limited to those directly supported by the triggered rules.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer with subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression
selected_rules.execute: do not use antidepressants routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: Do not use antidepressants routinely to treat subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression, due to the higher risk-benefit ratio at this level of depression severity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer
selected_rules.execute: screen for depression or distress
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with cancer should be screened for depression.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer
selected_rules.execute: link screening to effective interventions and use a clear diagnosis to guide treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: To improve health outcomes, screening must be linked to effective interventions. A clear diagnosis of depression is required to guide treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer with depression
selected_rules.execute: provide psychoeducation about depression in cancer
selected_rules.source_statement: Provide psychoeducation about the nature of depression in patients with cancer and consider providing handouts such as those published by the National Cancer Institute.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer with depression
selected_rules.execute: inform patients about the impact of depression on cancer outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Inform patients about the impact of depression on cancer outcomes, including reduced quality of life, intensification of physical symptoms, longer hospital stays, and reduced survival rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer with depression
selected_rules.execute: destigmatize clinical depression as a serious problem requiring treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Destigmatize clinical depression in cancer by framing it as a serious problem requiring treatment, rather than as a personal weakness or failure to cope.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer with depression
selected_rules.execute: investigate medical contributors such as hypothyroidism and vitamin B12, folate, or iron deficiency
selected_rules.source_statement: Investigate medical contributors to depression such as hypothyroidism, or vitamin B12, folate, or iron deficiency.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with cancer with depression
selected_rules.execute: assess and optimize cancer-related physical symptom control
selected_rules.source_statement: Assess and optimize cancer-related physical symptom control.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on palliative chemotherapy comes to clinic for worsening fatigue and low mood over the past 3 weeks. She reports feeling “down and overwhelmed,” with reduced enjoyment, poor sleep, and less appetite, but denies suicidal thoughts. Her symptoms are mild and she remains able to manage most daily activities, though she has been missing some social visits. She wonders if she should start an antidepressant right away because she feels emotionally drained by treatment. On review, her pain from bone metastases is not well controlled, and she also notes constipation and nausea. Recent labs show mild anemia, normal TSH, and no known B12 or folate testing yet.
recommended_actions: Do not use antidepressants routinely for her subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression.
Assess and optimize control of cancer-related physical symptoms, including her pain, constipation, and nausea.
generated_case.rationale: Her mood symptoms are described as mild/subthreshold rather than clear major depression, which triggers the recommendation against routine antidepressant use. She also has poorly controlled cancer-related physical symptoms, so optimizing symptom control is directly recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering NaF PET or choline PET
selected_rules.execute: Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of NaF and choline PET should be considered experimental

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering dietary advice for prostate cancer survivors
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: No diet plan can be recommended because no diet plan or food supplement has been associated with improved cancer outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A man is not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits and develops symptoms
selected_rules.execute: Perform a PSA test and consider symptom-specific diagnostic imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: In men who are not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits, a PSA test should be performed if the following symptoms develop. Additionally, diagnostic imaging specific to the patient's symptom(s) may be indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Men after primary therapy with long-lasting treatment effects
selected_rules.execute: Ensure follow-up providers are aware of quality-of-life domains affected and available management options
selected_rules.source_statement: Follow-up healthcare providers should be aware of the domains of quality of life potentially affected by treatment for prostate cancer and the management options available to combat them.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Following prostate cancer treatment when assessing quality of life
selected_rules.execute: Measure individual patient-reported outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: To ensure optimal quality of life in these men, individual patient-reported outcomes should be measured.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Prostate cancer survivor after completing curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide ongoing surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: For prostate cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any healthcare practitioner provides PSA surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Manage PSA according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway
selected_rules.source_statement: All healthcare practitioners that provide PSA surveillance should manage PSA as per the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Planning survivorship care models for prostate cancer survivors
selected_rules.execute: Include family physicians in survivorship care models
selected_rules.source_statement: Expert opinion supports family physicians being involved in all survivorship care models.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and has been followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits.
recommended_actions: Provide ongoing surveillance after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Manage PSA surveillance according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.
Measure individual patient-reported outcomes to assess quality of life.
Ensure follow-up providers are aware of the quality-of-life domains affected by treatment and the available management options.
Include the family physician in the survivorship care model.
Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental if considering them for recurrence/restaging.
Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette describes a prostate cancer survivor after curative-intent therapy, triggering the need for ongoing surveillance, PSA management per the CCO pathway, patient-reported outcome measurement, survivorship follow-up awareness of quality-of-life domains, and inclusion of the family physician. The patient also asks about recurrence imaging with PET and dietary supplements, which triggers the rules stating NaF and choline PET are experimental and that no specific diet or supplement should be recommended for improved cancer outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine monitoring or surveillance of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is not recommended for the routine monitoring and surveillance of lymphoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important and anatomical imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case and functional imaging is important
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important, anatomical imaging is equivocal, and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphomas, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early stage (I or II) Hodgkin lymphoma after 2 or 3 cycles of chemotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered as definitive single-modality therapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to assess early response and inform whether to complete therapy or give more therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the assessment of early response in early stage (I or II) HL following two or three cycles of chemotherapy when chemotherapy is being considered as the definitive single-modality therapy, to inform completion of therapy or if more therapy is warranted.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case with suspected recurrence and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to investigate recurrence of Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
selected_rules.source_statement: In potentially curable cases, when functional imaging is considered to be important and conventional imaging is equivocal, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended to investigate recurrence of HL or NHL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: residual mass after chemotherapy in Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma and further potentially curative therapy is being considered and biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to evaluate the residual mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the evaluation of residual mass(es) following chemotherapy in a patient with HL or NHL when further, potentially curative, therapy (such as radiation or stem cell transplantation) is being considered and when biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: FDG PET is abnormal during initial diagnosis of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Use clinical follow-up or biopsy to confirm findings because false positives can occur.
selected_rules.source_statement: FDG PET may disclose higher rates of systemic disease; however, due to false-positive results, FDG PET scans should be subject to clinical follow-up or biopsy.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP eight months ago and achieved a complete metabolic response. She now returns for a routine follow-up visit without B symptoms, palpable adenopathy, or abnormal laboratory findings. Her oncologist notes that she feels well and asks whether she should have an FDG PET/CT scan to “make sure nothing is coming back.” There is no new clinical concern, and prior post-treatment imaging was reassuring. The main question is whether to continue scheduled surveillance imaging or use PET/CT as a routine monitoring test.
recommended_actions: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance of lymphoma.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes routine monitoring/surveillance after lymphoma treatment without new symptoms or equivocal findings, which matches the rule against routine FDG PET/CT surveillance.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When providing palliative care across the disease course
selected_rules.execute: Use a team-based approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms and supports patients and families.
selected_rules.source_statement: Palliative care focuses on care that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms; it can be delivered throughout the course of the disease; it involves a team of healthcare providers and supports patients as well as their families.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting palliative care guidelines for review
selected_rules.execute: Include only papers that provide recommendations on palliative care and exclude papers that only describe guideline or consensus methods without recommendations.
selected_rules.source_statement: Papers were included if they provided any recommendations on palliative care. Papers were excluded if they only provided guideline or consensus methods and no recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When screening palliative care guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Restrict inclusion to guidelines published from 2011 to 2016.
selected_rules.source_statement: Due to the large number of guidelines, the Working Group decided to restrict the review to include only guidelines published in the past five years (2011 to 2016).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting higher-quality guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Include only guidelines with an AGREE Rigour of Development subsection score of 28 or higher.
selected_rules.source_statement: To identify the best-quality guidelines, the AGREE Instrument Rigour of Development scale was used and only guidelines that scored 28 or above were included in this review.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using the broad palliative care guideline for adults
selected_rules.execute: Apply the guideline covering the palliative care path from first diagnosis of serious illness to death.
selected_rules.source_statement: The "Palliative Care for Adults" guideline by McCusker et al. ... covers the palliative care path from the patient's first diagnosis of a serious illness to death.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When caring for patients in the last days and hours of life
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, decision support, care and management, education, and organization/policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This is a Canadian guideline about patient care in the last days and hours of life, and the recommendations in this guideline were specific to nurses. The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment for end of life, Decision support at end of life, Care and management at end of life, Education, and Organization and policy recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When assessing or preventing pressure ulcers in palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, planning, interventions, discharge or transfer/care arrangements, and education/organization-policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The topic of this guideline was the assessment and prevention of pressure ulcers. This guideline does not focus solely on palliative care, but the treatment and assessment of pressure ulcers in palliative care is a part of the guideline. ... The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment, Planning, Interventions, Discharge or transfer or the care arrangements, and Education and organization/policy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When managing chronic cough in patients receiving palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use simple linctus, then a therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, then an opioid or opioid derivative such as dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine.
selected_rules.source_statement: The recommendations include use of simple linctus, therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, and then prescription of an opioid or opioid derivative (dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine).

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities.
recommended_actions: Use a team-based palliative care approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms.
Provide support for both the patient and his family, including caregiver support and help with coping.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline across the disease course, from first diagnosis of serious illness through death.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because palliative care is being provided across the disease course and should address physical symptoms, emotional needs, and family support. It also triggers R005 because the patient has a serious illness and is being managed along the palliative care path from diagnosis onward. No other rules are directly relevant because he is not in the last days of life, and no pressure ulcer, chronic cough-specific, or guideline-selection scenario is described.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIB cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVA cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and randomized clinical trials are available
selected_rules.execute: offer participation in randomized clinical trials evaluating systemic therapy regimens
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that all patients with recurrent, metastatic or persistent cervical cancer be offered the opportunity to participate in randomized clinical trials, if available, that evaluate the efficacy of and adverse effects of systemic therapy regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: use cisplatin with paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Cisplatin with paclitaxel is recommended for this patient population

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and a cisplatin combination is being used
selected_rules.execute: substitute carboplatin for cisplatin when appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: The substitution of carboplatin for cisplatin in these combinations is also recommended for this target population because carboplatin is associated with fewer adverse effects and greater ease of administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVB, recurrent, or persistent cervical cancer not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy, performance status 0 to 1, adequate renal hepatic and bone marrow function, and no prior chemotherapy for recurrence, nonhealing wounds, active bleeding, or inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism
selected_rules.execute: use bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Bevacizumab in combination with cisplatin-paclitaxel is recommended for a specific subset of the target population, which includes only patients that match the characteristics of the GOG-0240 study population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and carboplatin-paclitaxel is feasible to administer
selected_rules.execute: use carboplatin-paclitaxel as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on these results, and on the feasibility of administration, carboplatin-paclitaxel is recommended as a treatment option for recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: consider cisplatin-vinorelbine
selected_rules.source_statement: cisplatin in other combinations, including cisplatin-vinorelbine, cisplatin-gemcitabine, and cisplatin-topotecan may also be considered.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain, hydronephrosis, and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
recommended_actions: Offer participation in a randomized clinical trial evaluating systemic therapy regimens, if the patient is interested and eligible.
Use cisplatin with paclitaxel as systemic therapy.
Carboplatin-paclitaxel is also a feasible treatment option if administration considerations favor carboplatin.
generated_case.rationale: The patient has recurrent cervical cancer with systemic therapy indicated, so she meets criteria for cisplatin-paclitaxel and for consideration of carboplatin-paclitaxel as an alternative option. Because randomized clinical trials are available, she should also be offered trial participation. Bevacizumab is not triggered because the vignette does not establish the specific GOG-0240-eligible subset, and no stage IIIB/IVA exclusion is needed here.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease is being diagnosed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for diagnosis
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma stage I, IIa, or IIb
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has suspected brain metastases from melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to diagnose brain metastases
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET or PET/CT is not recommended for the diagnosis of brain metastases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to detect primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the detection of primary uveal malignant melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and is high-risk with potentially resectable disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging of high-risk patients with potentially resectable disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, an isolated metastasis is identified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, metastectomy is being contemplated
selected_rules.execute: Use PET before metastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Assessing treatment response in malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation for or against PET
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in malignant melanoma due to insufficient evidence.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. He has no neurologic symptoms, no known distant disease, and his primary tumor was otherwise consistent with stage II disease. His oncology team is discussing whether PET imaging should be ordered to further characterize the nodal finding before deciding on additional management.
recommended_actions: Do not use PET for diagnosis of the sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease.
Do not use PET for staging of this melanoma presentation.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule for melanoma with sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease being diagnosed. The guideline states PET is not recommended for diagnosing sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging stage I, IIa, or IIb melanoma. No other rule is clearly triggered because the case does not describe high-risk potentially resectable disease, recurrence, isolated metastasis, or treatment-response assessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surgery plus radiation therapy as the initial local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone and surgery is planned
selected_rules.execute: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: Both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans should be taken into consideration for surgical planning.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider surgery alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
selected_rules.source_statement: The local treatment for an individual patient should be decided by a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient after consideration of the following: 1) patient characteristics (e.g., age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and existing comorbidities), 2) the potential benefit compared with the potential complications from surgery and/or toxicities associated with RT, and 3) patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: complete tumour resection is impossible
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as the optimal local treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: If complete tumour resection is impossible, RT alone may be the optimal choice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: postoperative residual tumour or positive margins
selected_rules.execute: Consider postoperative radiation therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: RT may be a treatment option postoperatively in patients who have residual tumours or positive margins.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone, surgery is planned, and there is good chemotherapy response in certain anatomic locations
selected_rules.execute: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: In certain anatomic locations with good chemotherapy response, the post-chemotherapy MRI may be the appropriate imaging modality to plan surgical resection margins.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked radiographic and symptomatic response. Repeat MRI shows substantial shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components, while the pre-chemotherapy MRI demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement. The orthopedic oncologist is planning limb-sparing surgery, but the lesion’s location near the metaphysis raises concern about achieving clear margins without excessive functional loss. The case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with the patient and family, who prefer an approach that preserves function if oncologically safe. The team notes that complete resection appears feasible, though the final extent of surgery will depend on careful comparison of the pre- and post-chemotherapy MRI studies.
recommended_actions: Take both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to help plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.

Decide local treatment at a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, weighing age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches localized Ewing’s sarcoma of bone with surgery planned after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, triggering preoperative staging and planning rules. The good chemotherapy response and an anatomic location where margin planning is important support using the post-chemotherapy MRI for resection margins. The multidisciplinary decision-making rule is also triggered because local treatment should be individualized with the patient and team.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent
selected_rules.execute: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
selected_rules.source_statement: Planned sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are both acceptable standards of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference
selected_rules.source_statement: Therefore, choice of treatment should be made on a caseby-case basis based on considerations that include patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the results of this systematic review, patients should have access to all effective cytotoxic drugs using a sequential strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening visceral disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider upfront combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy may be more appropriate for patients with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic or bulky life-threatening visceral disease given their higher overall response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: A meta-analysis of five trials (1-5) demonstrates a survival advantage for combination chemotherapy (HR, 0.92; 95%CI, 0.86-0.99, p=0.02).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that the survival advantage of combination chemotherapy is likely very small and may not be clinically significant
selected_rules.source_statement: Median survival advantage in most trials is 3 to 6 weeks (range <1 week to 12 weeks). Therefore, any survival advantage that exists is likely to be very small and not clinically significant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4), febrile neutropenia (1) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, but he is anxious to start treatment quickly. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that both sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are reasonable options in this palliative setting, and notes that the survival difference between approaches is likely small. They also discuss that combination therapy may increase the risk of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia.
recommended_actions: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care.
Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent, triggering R001. The case also supports the general metastatic setting for R005, R007, and R008. No rule about rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening disease was used, since those features were not present.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation outside a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous stem cell transplantation is not recommended for patients with MDS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend autologous SCT outside of a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: One systematic review comprising a total of 22 studies did not detect any benefit associated with autologous SCT (ASCT), and does not recommend it outside of a clinical trial (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: ASCT is not recommended for patients with AML in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
selected_rules.source_statement: While associated with more favourable treatment-related mortality (TRM) rates, if longterm survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as supported therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of ASCT for patients with AML in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission and long-term survival is the primary outcome
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT for long-term survival benefit.
selected_rules.source_statement: If long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk or poor-risk disease
selected_rules.execute: Use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered.
selected_rules.source_statement: Significant benefits in favour of alloSCT for both intermediate and poor risk patients (p<0.01) were detected, but no difference was detected with good risk patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML who achieves a second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Recommend allogeneic transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic transplantation is the recommended option for patients with AML who achieve a second or subsequent remission.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk.
