The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control.
recommended_actions: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred post-remission strategy.
Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult patient with AML in first complete remission and intermediate-risk disease, triggering the rules against autologous SCT in CR1 and the recommendation favoring allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered. The patient’s concern about long-term disease control also aligns with the rule stating there is no evidence to support autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario
selected_rules.execute: do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Also, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy has not been approved for patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all adult patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: use the TNM staging system
selected_rules.source_statement: The TNM staging system should be used for all patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours undergoing surgery
selected_rules.execute: aim to achieve complete resection
selected_rules.source_statement: The aim of surgery in all cases is to achieve a complete resection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: discuss the patient at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, including higher-volume centres when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Discussion of all patients at multidisciplinary cancer conference (MCC) is strongly recommended, not just at local MCC but also with higher-volume centres.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board
selected_rules.source_statement: Presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board should be considered.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment.
recommended_actions: Use the TNM staging system for this patient.
Discuss the case at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, and include a higher-volume centre when possible.
Do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment for this thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the general thymic epithelial tumour rules for all adult patients and the Ontario-specific restriction. The patient has a confirmed thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario, so TNM staging applies, multidisciplinary review is strongly recommended, and peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is not an approved treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with previously untreated, symptomatic, endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) alone is more effective for palliation of respiratory symptoms than HDREB alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use routine HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence does not provide conclusive results to suggest that routine use of HDREB and EBRT would provide improved symptom relief over EBRT alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction
selected_rules.execute: Use a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB
selected_rules.source_statement: December 2022: the recommendation "For patients with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction, a surgical core out procedure may be needed before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB" is no longer endorsed because the updated evidence does not show a benefit of this procedure.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient previously treated by EBRT who is symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB if technically feasible
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients previously treated by EBRT who are symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease, HDREB is recommended, providing that endobronchial brachytherapy is technically feasible.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease where selected combined treatment is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider HDREB plus EBRT in selected patients
selected_rules.source_statement: June 2018: It is the opinion of the Lung Cancer Disease Site Group that HDREB and EBRT might be suitable in selected patients.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but not complete lung collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The oncology team is considering whether to add endobronchial brachytherapy to external beam radiation, but there is no prior EBRT history and no clear reason to prefer a combined approach over radiation alone.
recommended_actions: Use external beam radiation therapy alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms rather than HDREB alone.
Do not routinely use HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone.
Consider HDREB plus EBRT only in selected patients, recognizing this is an opinion-based option rather than a routine recommendation.
generated_case.rationale: The patient has previously untreated, symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer, which triggers the rules addressing initial palliation. EBRT alone is favored over HDREB alone, and routine combined HDREB plus EBRT is not supported by conclusive evidence. A combined approach may be considered only selectively.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Ipilimumab is not recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of interferon alpha (IFN-α) for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma outside of a clinical trial is no longer recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma
selected_rules.execute: do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, and isolated limb perfusion are not recommended for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma except as part of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and no BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma without BRAF V600E or V600K mutations with high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations and high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.execute: perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions
selected_rules.source_statement: Molecular testing of high-risk melanoma patients to characterize mutations should be conducted to help guide appropriate treatment decisions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with clinically positive lymph nodes before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: These trials and recommendations regarding axillary resection do not apply to patients with clinically positive lymph nodes (by palpation or radiologic investigation), and the standard of care is dissection of lymph nodes in that area (axillary, groin, or head and neck) prior to adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab, or targeted therapy if an actionable mutation is identified, as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy (in patients with identified mutations) are recommended for adjuvant therapy of mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence (stage III). He is recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. He has no known BRAF V600E or V600K mutation status yet, and no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging. He is being seen to discuss adjuvant treatment options after surgery.
recommended_actions: Do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment outside a clinical trial.
Do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guérin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial.
Perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to help guide adjuvant treatment decisions.
generated_case.rationale: The patient has completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high-risk features, which triggers the non-recommendations against ipilimumab, interferon alpha, and other older adjuvant approaches. Because he is a high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant therapy, molecular testing is also recommended to determine mutation status and guide treatment selection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has undergone initial complete resection of GIST
selected_rules.execute: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, there is insufficient evidence to support the use of imatinib as an adjuvant therapy in patients who have undergone initial complete resection of disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering neoadjuvant treatment with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Do not use neoadjuvant imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, the use of neoadjuvant imatinib is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Use imatinib as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST, treatment with imatinib is a recommended therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: starting imatinib for KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe imatinib 400 mg daily as the initial dose
selected_rules.source_statement: Until additional data become available, the initial dose of imatinib should be prescribed at a dose of 400 mg daily.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with stable disease on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib until disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with stable disease should be treated until disease progression.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient develops serious toxicity on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Discontinue imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: Treatment should be discontinued if serious toxicity develops.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient achieves complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib indefinitely until further data define optimal duration
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients who achieve a complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib, treatment should be continued indefinitely until further data is available regarding the optimum duration of therapy in such patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Apply these recommendations to this population
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+).

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual or metastatic disease on postoperative imaging, and the tumor was not being treated as unresectable or metastatic disease. He feels well and is eager to do anything that might lower recurrence risk, but he has not started any systemic therapy yet.
recommended_actions: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy after initial complete resection of GIST.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches the rule for patients who have undergone initial complete resection of GIST. The guideline states there is insufficient evidence to support adjuvant imatinib in this setting, so imatinib should not be used postoperatively.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for allogeneic SCT
selected_rules.execute: Do not use allogeneic stem cell transplantation for AL amyloidosis
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no evidence supporting the use of allogeneic SCT for patients with AL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for treatment including bone marrow or stem cell transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation as an option for selected patients, preferably in an investigative setting
selected_rules.source_statement: High-dose chemotherapy (CT) and autologous SCT is an option for selected patients with primary systemic amyloidosis, preferably within an investigative setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use autologous SCT only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals
selected_rules.source_statement: In consideration of the lack of curative treatment options and the limitations of the evidence reviewed, the Expert Panel believes that offering ASCT may be a reasonable option for some patients, depending on performance status, co-morbidities, patient preferences, and ultimate treatment goals.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: The patient selection process and the ultimate decision to perform an SCT should take into account not only disease-related characteristics, but also comorbidities and patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality
selected_rules.source_statement: Careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function is an emerging concept in amyloidosis that should be considered to reduce transplant-related mortality.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area and that first relapse transplantation may be considered as an emerging area pending new evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on how newer agents affect the need for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Newer agents are being investigated in the treatment of AL amyloidosis. At this time it is not known how they may impact the need for SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on transplantation outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis presents after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. Because he has AL amyloidosis and is being evaluated for SCT, the team reviews his organ involvement, overall functional status, comorbidities, and treatment goals before deciding whether he is an appropriate transplant candidate.
recommended_actions: Use autologous stem cell transplantation only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals.
Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences.
Consider careful patient selection based on the degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT, which triggers the rules supporting selective use of autologous SCT and emphasizing individualized assessment. He is clinically stable and functionally well, so the vignette appropriately highlights evaluation of performance status, comorbidities, disease burden, organ function, and patient preferences before proceeding.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use re-irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: However, re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy as equally effective treatment options
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with early (T1) glottic cancer, recommended treatment options include the equally effective endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose between surgery and radiation based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition
selected_rules.source_statement: The choice between treatment modalities should be based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and anterior commissure involvement
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Anterior commissure involvement may be a factor that favours a recommendation of radiotherapy over surgery due to a common opinion that voice outcomes are particularly affected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and tumor localized to the midportion of the vocal fold with uncompromised endoscopic accessibility
selected_rules.execute: Consider endolaryngeal surgery as an ideal candidate treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Tumours localized to the midportion of the vocal fold, and where endoscopic accessibility is uncompromised, may be considered ideal candidates for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot tolerate a general anaesthetic
selected_rules.execute: Prefer radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Other important practical considerations include the ability for patients to tolerate a general anaesthetic, which is required for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot cooperate with daily treatment for four to six weeks
selected_rules.execute: Avoid radiation therapy and consider surgery if otherwise appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: In contrast, radiotherapy requires patient cooperation for daily treatment for four to six weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and local recurrence after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Consider partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery, is possible for local recurrence following surgery.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 9 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy and biopsy confirm a small local recurrence confined to the glottis, still staged as early T1 disease. He remains otherwise well and asks whether another course of radiation could treat the recurrence. He is anxious to preserve voice but understands that the lesion is limited and potentially amenable to local salvage treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not use re-irradiation for this recurrence.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with early T1 glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy, which triggers the rule that re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving conventional fractionation radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not escalate dose beyond 66 Gy
selected_rules.source_statement: Dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use hyperfractionated radiotherapy regimens that do not accelerate the treatment course
selected_rules.source_statement: Hyperformated radiotherapy regimens that do not result in acceleration of the treatment course, even though the total nominal radiotherapy dose may be modestly increased, are not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with unresectable stage III NSCLC is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not routinely give induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Routine use of induction chemotherapy prior to concurrent chemoradiotherapy is not recommended;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for durvalumab after chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: do not exclude durvalumab solely because of EGFR or PD-L1 biomarker status
selected_rules.source_statement: At this time, it is inappropriate to exclude patients from therapy based solely on a biomarker such as epidermal growth factor receptor or programmed death-ligand 1 status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC (N2 or N3) and curative-intent treatment is planned
selected_rules.execute: use concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Concurrent chemoradiation should be used for curative-intent treatment of patients with unresectable, lymph node-positive (N2 or N3) stage III NSCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: A standard dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy given in fractions of 2 Gy once per day over six weeks is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage III NSCLC and has not progressed after chemoradiation and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: use durvalumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Durvalumab should be used in patients with stage III NSCLC who have not progressed postchemoradiation and have no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is having chemoradiotherapy and surgery for stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: schedule surgery three to five weeks after chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC who are having chemoradiotherapy and surgery, ensure that their surgery is scheduled for three to five weeks after the chemoradiotherapy.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions over six weeks with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated to improve local control, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
recommended_actions: Use concurrent chemoradiation for curative-intent treatment of this unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC.
Use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks.
Do not escalate the radiation dose beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches unresectable, node-positive stage III NSCLC planned for curative treatment, which triggers concurrent chemoradiation. Because the patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation, standard fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy once daily is recommended. The question of dose escalation is also addressed by the rules: conventional fractionation should not be escalated beyond 66 Gy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering adding gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is not recommended for adjuvant chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 65 years and capecitabine is being considered instead of adjuvant AC or CMF
selected_rules.execute: do not use capecitabine as the adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of AC or CMF
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients older than 65 years, capecitabine is not recommended as an adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of adjuvant AC or CMF (oral cyclophosphamide).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen
selected_rules.execute: do not treat ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours (treated with or without chemotherapy) the addition of ovarian ablation or suppression to tamoxifen is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: do not use ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours, treated with or without chemotherapy, ovarian ablation or suppression plus five years of an AI is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient does not have HER2-positive breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Only patients with HER2+ breast cancer (IHC 3+, ISH ratio ≥2, or 6+ HER2 gene copies per cell nucleus) should be offered adjuvant trastuzumab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving trastuzumab and has any of the following: LVEF under 55%, documented congestive heart failure, high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias, angina requiring medication, clinically significant valvular disease, transmural infarction on ECG, or poorly controlled hypertension
selected_rules.execute: do not offer trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: trastuzumab should not be offered to patients with any of the following: a left ventricular ejection fraction LVEF of <55%; a history of documented congestive heart failure; high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias; angina pectoris requiring medication; clinically significant valvular disease; evidence of transmural infarction on electrocardiograph (ECG); poorly controlled hypertension.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Ki-67 for routine risk stratification in early-stage breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter outside clinical trials
selected_rules.source_statement: It is premature to recommend its use as a standard parameter for patient risk stratification, although it may be evaluated in clinical trials.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use intrinsic subtypes outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: At this point, the use of these subtypes in clinical decision making outside of a clinical trial is not recommended.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is being seen to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 1+. She is postmenopausal and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
Do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab because the cancer is not HER2-positive.
Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette is designed to trigger rules about adjuvant chemotherapy intensification, HER2-directed therapy, and biomarker use. Because the tumor is HER2-negative, trastuzumab is not recommended. The proposed addition of capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is also not recommended. Ki-67 and intrinsic subtype testing are mentioned only as decision aids, but both are not recommended for routine clinical decision making outside trials.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The care of patients with head and neck cancer should be coordinated among members of an experienced Core Team, comprised of a group of physicians and allied healthcare providers who will be responsible for the assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: The Primary Care Provider will be responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient and will offer supportive care after treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: involve an extended team as needed to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The Extended Team will be called upon by the core team to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: members of the care team
selected_rules.execute: ensure team members have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers
selected_rules.source_statement: Members of the Teams must have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: recognize the primary care physician as not involved in day-to-day treatment but important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health
selected_rules.source_statement: The primary care physician is not involved in the day to day treatment of the head and neck cancer patient but plays an important role in post-treatment supportive care and is responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours
selected_rules.execute: use reconstruction expertise from a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Reconstruction expertise is required for the surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours and necessitates a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting head and neck malignancies
selected_rules.execute: use CAP-CCO standards for pathology reporting
selected_rules.source_statement: Pathologists are advised to use the CAP-CCO standards for reporting head and neck malignancies (5).

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting nodal dissections
selected_rules.execute: include levels and structures in the specimen, number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of nodes, and presence and location of extracapsular spread
selected_rules.source_statement: The reporting of nodal dissections should include a description of the levels and structures included in the specimen, including number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of these nodes, and the presence and location of extracapsular spread of tumour.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and remains responsible for his general medical care and supportive follow-up after cancer treatment. Because he may need surgery depending on response to therapy, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise would be required if an operation becomes necessary.
recommended_actions: Coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation.
Ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient with head and neck cancer, which triggers the general care-coordination recommendations. The multidisciplinary clinic and planned follow-up align with the need for an experienced core team and ongoing primary care involvement. No pathology-reporting or surgical-management-specific rule was selected as a triggered rule.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering radiotherapy alone given preoperatively or postoperatively
selected_rules.execute: Do not use radiotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for radiotherapy given alone, either preoperatively or postoperatively, compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering postoperative chemotherapy alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not use postoperative chemotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for postoperative chemotherapy given alone compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy is recommended as the preferred modality for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Consider preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy alone as an alternative treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone is an alternative choice for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Prefer preoperative chemoradiotherapy over surgery alone when choosing preoperative treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the majority of the evidence available at this time, the GI DSG believes that preoperative chemoradiotherapy for resectable carcinoma of the esophagus is the preferred approach.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that either preoperative chemotherapy or preoperative chemoradiotherapy may provide survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The GI DSG acknowledges there is evidence indicating survival benefits with either preoperative chemotherapy or chemoradiotherapy compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before administering preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinicians should recognize that the survival advantage of preoperative therapy may be minimal and a discussion with patients regarding potential adverse effects is required.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Deciding whether to give preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Decisions to administer preoperative therapy should be based on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but resectable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment. He has well-controlled hypertension and no major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options and discussing expected toxicities and the fact that any survival benefit may be modest. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
recommended_actions: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality.
Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal.
Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer for whom surgery is appropriate, triggering the recommendation for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy. Because preoperative therapy is being considered, the team should discuss adverse effects and recognize that the survival advantage may be minimal. The choice should also be individualized based on preferences, comorbidities, and fitness for trimodality therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases and is receiving a BMA
selected_rules.execute: do not use the BMA alone as the only treatment for bone pain
selected_rules.source_statement: The analgesic effects of BMAs are modest, and they should not be used alone for bone pain.

selected_rules.activation_condition: monitoring BMA use with biochemical markers
selected_rules.execute: do not use biochemical markers to monitor BMA use routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: this ASCO guideline update does not alter the prior recommendation (No. 7) that the use of the biochemical markers to monitor BMA use is not recommended for routine care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has breast cancer with evidence of bone metastases
selected_rules.execute: treat with a bone-modifying agent (BMA)
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with breast cancer who have evidence of bone metastases should be treated with BMAs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with denosumab
selected_rules.execute: use denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include denosumab, 120 mg subcutaneously, every 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with pamidronate
selected_rules.execute: use pamidronate 90 mg intravenously every 3 to 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include ... pamidronate, 90 mg intravenously, every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: use zoledronic acid 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include ... zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is receiving zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: administer zoledronic acid over no less than 15 minutes
selected_rules.source_statement: If patients are treated with zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously administered over no less than 15 minutes, dosing options are every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases
selected_rules.execute: apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management
selected_rules.source_statement: The Update Committee recommends that the current standard of care for supportive care and pain management-analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management-be applied.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer presents with worsening deep aching pain in her pelvis and lower back. Imaging shows multiple lytic bone metastases without spinal cord compression. She is already receiving denosumab for skeletal-related event prevention. Despite the agent, her pain has progressed over the past month and is limiting sleep and walking. She has been using acetaminophen with only partial relief. Her oncology team is considering additional pain-directed treatment and referral for symptom control. She asks whether the bone-targeted therapy alone should be enough for the pain.
recommended_actions: Do not use the bone-modifying agent alone as the only treatment for her bone pain.
Apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management.
generated_case.rationale: She has bone pain from bone metastases and is receiving a bone-modifying agent, which triggers the recommendation against using the BMA alone for pain control. Her pain from bone metastases also triggers the recommendation to use standard supportive care and pain management measures.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering changing management based on HPV test results outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing except in the context of a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Altering management decisions based on results from HPV testing is not recommended beyond the context of a clinical trial at this time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using HPV PCR testing alone
selected_rules.execute: Avoid qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone should be avoided.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
selected_rules.execute: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: The tumours of all adult patients presenting with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas should be routinely tested for HPV status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary
selected_rules.execute: Routinely test the neck nodal tissue for HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that the neck nodal tissue of patients with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary be routinely tested for HPV status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV testing is being performed for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
selected_rules.execute: Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that HPV status in oropharyngeal SCC be initially determined using immunohistochemical (IHC) staining for p16.

selected_rules.activation_condition: p16 staining shows cytoplasmic and nuclear staining, is moderate to strong and diffuse, and is present in at least 70% of tumour cells
selected_rules.execute: Consider the p16 result positive
selected_rules.source_statement: IHC staining for p16 can be considered positive when the following three criteria are met: cytoplasmic and nuclear staining; staining is moderate to strong and diffuse; staining is present in at least 70% of tumour cells.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV testing is being done in the pathology laboratory
selected_rules.execute: Perform analysis on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns
selected_rules.source_statement: Analysis should be performed on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV-status testing is being performed
selected_rules.execute: Carry out testing in licensed and accredited laboratories
selected_rules.source_statement: All testing should be carried out in licensed and accredited laboratories.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive sore throat, dysphagia to solids, and a painless left neck mass. Biopsy of the tonsillar lesion shows squamous cell carcinoma, and the pathology team plans HPV-status testing to help characterize the tumor. The specimen is being processed in a licensed, accredited laboratory using paraffin-embedded tissue sections cut at 4 microns. Initial p16 immunohistochemical staining shows diffuse, moderate-to-strong cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in about 80% of tumour cells. The oncologist asks whether the HPV result should change the treatment plan immediately.
recommended_actions: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status because this is an adult with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status.
Consider the p16 result positive because the staining is cytoplasmic and nuclear, moderate to strong and diffuse, and present in at least 70% of tumour cells.
Perform the testing on paraffin-block sections or unstained slides cut at 4 microns in a licensed and accredited laboratory.
Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing outside the context of a clinical trial.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches adult oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, so routine HPV testing is indicated. Because HPV testing is being performed for oropharyngeal SCC, p16 IHC is the recommended initial test. The described staining pattern meets the positivity criteria. The laboratory and specimen handling also match the testing recommendations. Although the HPV result is available, management should not be changed on the basis of HPV testing outside a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering PET-CT during follow-up
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Positron emission tomography (PET) with computed tomography (PET-CT),

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering other imaging tests in an asymptomatic patient
selected_rules.execute: Do not use other imaging tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering biomarker tests in an asymptomatic patient
selected_rules.execute: Do not use biomarker tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient was treated with radiotherapy and is in follow-up beyond five years
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform cytological follow-up.
selected_rules.source_statement: Cytological follow-up is not recommended for patients who have been treated with radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is clinically disease-free and asymptomatic after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
selected_rules.source_statement: This practice guideline applies to women who are clinically disease free and asymptomatic after receiving potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer. This guideline does not apply to the follow-up of women who have been treated for cervical precancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: providing follow-up care after primary treatment for cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
selected_rules.source_statement: Follow-up care after primary treatment should be conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in the surveillance of patients with cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: during follow-up care after primary treatment
selected_rules.execute: Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
selected_rules.source_statement: Continuity of care and dialogue between the healthcare professional and patient about symptoms of recurrence may enhance and facilitate early cancer recurrence detection because the majority of women who develop a recurrence have symptoms and signs that occur outside scheduled follow-up visits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: follow-up within the first 5 years after treatment
selected_rules.execute: Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
selected_rules.source_statement: A reasonable follow-up strategy involves visits at the following intervals: o every three to four months within the first two years, o every six to 12 months from years 3 to 5.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman returns for surveillance 18 months after completing chemoradiation for stage IIB cervical cancer. She is clinically disease-free, reports no vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, weight loss, or bowel/bladder changes, and her exam is unremarkable. Her oncologist is discussing whether to order a PET-CT because she feels anxious about recurrence, but the patient has no symptoms suggestive of relapse. The team reviews her follow-up plan and notes that she is still within the first two years after treatment. She asks how often she should be seen and what warning signs should prompt an earlier visit.
recommended_actions: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
generated_case.rationale: She is an asymptomatic, clinically disease-free patient after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer, so the surveillance guideline applies. Because she is considering PET-CT during routine follow-up, that test is not recommended. Follow-up should be coordinated by a clinician experienced in cancer surveillance, with ongoing communication about recurrence symptoms. Since she is within the first two years after treatment, visits every 3 to 4 months are appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient at elevated risk of clinically significant prostate cancer and a candidate for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: MPMRI is recommended prior to biopsy in patients who are candidates for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a positive multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI-targeted biopsy together with transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is positive, MPMRI-TB and TRUS-SB should be performed together to maximize detection of CSPCa.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient had a prior negative transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy and is at high risk of clinically significant prostate cancer with curative management being considered
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients who had a prior negative TRUS-SB and demonstrate a high risk of having CSPCa in whom curative management is being considered: MPMRI should be performed,

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient had a prior negative transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy and multiparametric MRI is positive
selected_rules.execute: Perform targeted biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is positive, targeted biopsy should be performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a negative multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Consider forgoing biopsy after discussing risks and benefits with the patient and arranging ongoing follow-up
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is negative, consider forgoing any biopsy after discussion of the risks and benefits with the patient as part of shared decision making and ongoing followup.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a negative multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient that a negative multiparametric MRI may miss clinically significant prostate cancer
selected_rules.source_statement: Between 8% and 24% of patients with CSPCa may be missed by a negative MPMRI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering multiparametric MRI for a biopsy-naive patient
selected_rules.execute: Use multiparametric MRI only if high-quality interpretation is available and operators have experience performing targeted biopsies
selected_rules.source_statement: MPMRI should only be performed if there is availability of high-quality MPMRI interpretation and operators with experience performing targeted biopsies (see Recommendation 3).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering multiparametric MRI for a biopsy-naive patient
selected_rules.execute: Use multiparametric MRI only when there is intent for curative management if biopsy is positive
selected_rules.source_statement: Due to the limited availability, MPMRI is recommended only for patients where there is intent of curative management should the biopsy be positive for CSPCa.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with a PSA of 11.8 ng/mL and an abnormal digital rectal exam showing mild left-sided induration. He has never had a prostate biopsy. He is otherwise healthy, has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years, and says he would want curative treatment if clinically significant prostate cancer is found. His urologist considers him at elevated risk for clinically significant, clinically localized prostate cancer and is planning diagnostic workup before biopsy.
recommended_actions: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy.
generated_case.rationale: This biopsy-naive patient has elevated risk of clinically significant prostate cancer and is a candidate for curative management with suspected clinically localized disease, which matches the indication for pre-biopsy multiparametric MRI. No additional rule is triggered because MRI results are not yet known.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult or paediatric patient after allogeneic transplant with steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD
selected_rules.execute: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is an acceptable therapy for the treatment of steroid-dependent / refractory acute GVHD in adult and paediatric patients

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult or paediatric patient after allogeneic transplant with steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory chronic GVHD
selected_rules.execute: Use extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is an effective therapy for the treatment of steroid-dependent / refractory chronic GVHD in adult and paediatric patients

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has steroid-refractory acute GVHD with primarily refractory skin involvement
selected_rules.execute: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with primarily refractory skin GVHD should be considered for ECP treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has steroid-refractory GVHD and meets Ontario eligibility criteria
selected_rules.execute: Note that ECP is a covered therapy in Ontario
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is currently a covered therapy in Ontario for patients with steroid refractory GVHD who meet certain eligibility criteria

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old woman is 74 days after matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. She developed diffuse erythematous rash, watery diarrhea, and mild transaminitis consistent with acute GVHD. Despite 10 days of high-dose prednisone, her diarrhea improved only slightly and the rash persists, so the transplant team considers her acute GVHD steroid-refractory. She remains hemodynamically stable and has no active infection. Because the GVHD is not responding adequately to steroids, the team is discussing next-line therapy.
recommended_actions: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy for steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult after allogeneic transplant with steroid-refractory acute GVHD, which directly matches R001. No additional rules were triggered because chronic GVHD is not present, skin-predominant refractory disease is not the primary focus, and Ontario coverage criteria were not specified.

selected_rules.activation_condition: tumour pathology creates doubt
selected_rules.execute: Have the pathology reviewed by an expert
selected_rules.source_statement: A tumour pathology causing doubt should be reviewed by an expert.

selected_rules.activation_condition: restaging of a sub-optimally staged patient reveals more advanced disease
selected_rules.execute: Use chemotherapy as the preferred treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: If the restaging of a sub-optimally staged patient reveals a more advanced disease, chemotherapy is the preferred treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: lymph node sampling was not conducted as part of staging surgery
selected_rules.execute: Favor adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: A subgroup analysis of one randomized controlled trial demonstrated that if lymph node sampling is not conducted as part of the staging surgery then adjuvant chemotherapy is favoured in terms of overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has undergone optimal surgical staging including pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling and has stage I disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have undergone optimal surgical staging, including pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling, and have stage I disease may or may not benefit from adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging
selected_rules.execute: Offer re-operation to optimally define tumour stage and then give adjuvant therapy based on findings
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have not undergone optimal surgical staging can be offered two options. The first option is that they undergo re-operation to optimally define the tumour stage and then be offered adjuvant therapy based on the findings.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging
selected_rules.execute: Offer platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease recurrence risk and improve survival
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have not undergone optimal surgical staging can be offered two options. The other option is that they be offered platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease the risk of recurrence and improve survival.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman underwent surgery for a 4.2 cm epithelial ovarian tumor found incidentally during evaluation for bloating. The operative report notes hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, but no pelvic or para-aortic lymph node sampling was performed, so the staging was suboptimal. Final pathology suggests stage I disease, but the report also states that the tumor morphology is somewhat unusual and the pathologist is not fully confident about the exact histologic subtype. She is otherwise recovering well and asks whether she needs more treatment now or whether additional surgery would help clarify the stage.
recommended_actions: Have the pathology reviewed by an expert.
Offer re-operation to optimally define the tumour stage and then give adjuvant therapy based on findings.
Offer platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease recurrence risk and improve survival.
generated_case.rationale: The pathology report explicitly creates doubt about the tumour diagnosis, which triggers expert review. The patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging because lymph node sampling was omitted, which triggers the options of re-operation for definitive staging or platinum-based chemotherapy. These recommendations are directly supported by the selected rules.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old woman presents to a community health clinic for an overdue cervical cancer screening. Allow NPs to perform Pap smears as alternate providers to physicians.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has DCIS and is a candidate for breast-conserving surgery

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has DCIS and has undergone breast-conserving surgery

The patient is anxious about recurrence but strongly prefers to preserve her breast if oncologically appropriate. She asks about treatment options and whether radiation would still be needed after surgery.
recommended_actions: Offer her the choice of breast-conserving surgery or total mastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before preoperative therapy is planned

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. recommended_actions: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
Discuss the potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.

Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection for sentinel node metastasis.

generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches a patient with positive sentinel node metastasis in whom CLND is being compared with observation. The selected rules support observation over routine CLND because survival benefit has not been shown and adverse events are higher with CLND. Since the patient is managed with nodal observation, scheduled ultrasound and clinical follow-up are recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered

selected_rules.source_statement: There was no evidence to suggest any survival or local control benefit using sequential RTCT compared with RT alone. Given these results and the increased adverse effects associated with sequential RTCT, this approach should not be recommended in standard practice.

selected_rules.execute: Consider concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone

Because he is otherwise fit, the oncologist discusses a concomitant approach rather than radiotherapy alone. recommended_actions: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has positive surgical margins

selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has seminal vesicle invasion
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.execute: Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: On the basis of the overall trial findings as well as these subgroup analyses, it can be concluded that men found at radical prostatectomy to have any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion benefit from adjuvant RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy and the comparison is observation with salvage therapy reserved
selected_rules.execute: Prefer adjuvant radiotherapy over observation with salvage therapy reserved

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that his adverse pathologic features place him at higher risk of recurrence despite the undetectable PSA.
recommended_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy specimen is being handled in the operating room
selected_rules.execute: do not perform frozen section analysis for margin status

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is low-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is intermediate-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is high-risk
selected_rules.execute: offer referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC)
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk patients should be offered a referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC).

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins
selected_rules.source_statement: The main goals of RP are (a) complete eradication of the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins, (b) preservation of urinary function, and (c) preservation of erectile function, where appropriate, but, in some cases, it is not possible to achieve all three.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: balance margin rates with functional outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Radical Prostatectomy should aim at achieving a negative margin, while ensuring a balance between margin rates and functional outcomes

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach except in high-risk patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Sparing of the neurovascular bundles should be considered the "standard approach" except for high-risk patients.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The team discusses the operative goals, including complete removal of the cancer-containing organ and achieving negative margins while balancing functional outcomes. Because the patient is not considered high-risk, nerve-sparing is being considered as part of the standard approach. The specimen is then handed off for routine pathologic processing.
recommended_actions: Aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins.
Balance margin rates with functional outcomes.
Consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach, since the patient is not high-risk.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient undergoing radical prostatectomy, which triggers operative goals related to negative margins and functional preservation. The patient is not high-risk, so neurovascular bundle sparing is appropriately considered. No frozen section or apex-specific processing details are included, and no radiation referral is indicated because the patient is intermediate-risk and already selected surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has porphyria or known allergy to porphyrins

selected_rules.activation_condition: Tumor erodes into a major blood vessel

selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... existing tracheoesophageal fistulas.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives endobronchial PDT

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives PDT

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives Photofrin-based PDT
selected_rules.execute: Advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary NSCLC and medically inoperable early-stage disease accessible by bronchoscopy
