selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and proposed risk-adapted surveillance based on tumour size or rete testis invasion
selected_rules.execute: Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Include follow-up investigations of sites at risk of relapse

selected_rules.execute: Continue post-treatment monitoring for relapse; except after extended-field radiotherapy, follow-up should be as thorough as surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients treated with adjuvant therapy, post treatment monitoring for disease relapse is still necessary. Except in the specific case of extended-field radiotherapy, the follow-up after adjuvant therapy should be as thorough as the surveillance conducted in the absence of adjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and planned adjuvant therapy with fertility concerns

After discussion, he is told that surveillance is the preferred approach after orchidectomy, and that management should not be based on a risk-adapted strategy using tumour size or rete testis invasion. He agrees to proceed with surveillance and understands the need for regular follow-up visits and imaging.
recommended_actions: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy.
Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering routine allogeneic transplantation for multiple myeloma

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering planned double (tandem) autologous stem cell transplantation

selected_rules.source_statement: Planned double (tandem) ASCT cannot be recommended on the current evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering purging or selection of harvested stem cells
selected_rules.execute: Do not use currently available purging methods routinely

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with advanced-stage multiple myeloma and good performance status

selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous transplantation is recommended for patients with advanced-stage myeloma and good performance status.

He asks whether he should undergo stem cell transplant and whether he should receive a planned second transplant because a friend told him that “more is better.” His oncologist is also considering adding thalidomide to the upfront regimen and wonders if stem cells should be purged before collection. recommended_actions: Proceed with autologous transplantation; evidence is strongest in patients under 65 years old without significant renal dysfunction after hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy.
Refer for transplant assessment early and avoid extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan before stem cell collection.

generated_case.rationale: This patient matches the strongest evidence group for autologous transplantation: age under 65, preserved renal function, and completion of hydration plus induction therapy. Because stem cells have not yet been collected and he may be transplant-eligible, exposure to alkylating agents should be limited before collection. The vignette also raises two practices that are not routinely recommended on current evidence: planned tandem transplantation outside a trial and routine purging of harvested stem cells. selected_rules.activation_condition: accessory labeled single use or disposable

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing colonoscopy training

selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has not progressed on first-line treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has responded to first-line treatment

selected_rules.execute: Consider PCI because the recommendation strength is not strong

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use zoledronic acid at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with symptomatic mCRPC, Ra-223 is recommended to extend overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: This recommendation applies to men with predominantly bony metastases and no evidence of visceral metastases or large nodal metastases.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma is seen after staging workup for a rising PSA and abnormal MRI. Biopsy shows Gleason 9 disease with extracapsular extension, but CT and bone scan show no distant metastases. He has no bone pain, maintains a good performance status, and has not started androgen deprivation therapy yet. His creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bone-strengthening medication now to prevent cancer spread to the bones. He is otherwise healthy and takes only tamsulosin for urinary symptoms.
recommended_actions: Do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis in this patient with high-risk localized prostate cancer.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette describes a man with high-risk localized prostate cancer without metastatic disease, which matches the activation condition for R001. The rule specifically states bisphosphonates are not recommended for reducing the risk of first bone metastasis in this setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and is age 25
selected_rules.execute: do not start screening from age 25
selected_rules.source_statement: This (Ontario) Working Group, however, did not find justification for screening from age 25 in this group (or any of the other high risk groups).

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in target population risk category 1, 2, 3, or 5
selected_rules.execute: perform MRI in addition to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI in addition to mammography is recommended for women in target population risk categories 1, 2, 3, and 5 above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen annually
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the appropriate screening frequency, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually, as this was the frequency typical of the identified studies on which the recommendation for screening is based.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen from age 30 to 69 years
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the ages of patients who should be screened, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually from 30 to 69 years of age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30
selected_rules.execute: use MRI in addition to mammography because benefits outweigh harms
selected_rules.source_statement: It is the opinion of the Working Group that the benefits of MRI in terms of increased sensitivity outweigh the potential harms of higher recall rates and biopsy rates for all women in risk category 5 who received ≥20 Gy radiation before the age of 30.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 4 with high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer plus breast density at least 50% and family history of breast cancer but not enough to be category 3
selected_rules.execute: consider adding MRI to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to risk category 4, there are preliminary data that a subgroup of patients in risk category 4 might benefit from the addition of MRI to mammography (e.g., women who in addition to a high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer, also have breast density ≥50% and a family history of breast cancer though insufficient to put them in category 3).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is in risk category 4
selected_rules.execute: do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening for patients in risk category 4.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin screening now, since she has heard that high-risk women need early imaging. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she falls into a high-risk group, but also notes that she is only 25 years old. The discussion focuses on what screening, if any, should be started at this age and whether MRI should be added to mammography later on.
recommended_actions: Do not start screening from age 25.
If/when screening is undertaken in risk category 5 patients who received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30, use MRI in addition to mammography because the benefits outweigh the potential harms.
generated_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient is a woman in risk category 5 and is age 25, and the rule explicitly says not to start screening from age 25. R005 is also triggered because she is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30; for this group, the Working Group considers MRI in addition to mammography to have benefits that outweigh harms.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor develops symptoms suggestive of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Evaluate according to the symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: Survivors who develop symptoms suggestive of recurrence, should be evaluated according to those symptoms.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for SCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has late side effects of curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide support to help manage symptoms and improve quality of life
selected_rules.source_statement: Health-related quality of life (QoL) is very important for long-term survivors suffering from late side effects of their curative-intent therapy (including surgery, chemotherapy and radiation therapy). The following is a summary of issues reported by survivors. Health care professionals need to aid lung cancer survivors in handling these symptoms to improve QoL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide surveillance follow-up care
selected_rules.source_statement: For lung cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required and may be provided by specialists, family physicians or hospital-based nurses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy for NSCLC or SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Provide smoking cessation counselling
selected_rules.source_statement: Smoking cessation counselling is recommended for patients who have completed curativeintent therapy for NSCLC and SCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Providing smoking cessation advice to a lung cancer survivor
selected_rules.execute: Use behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Although verbal cessation advice from a health care professional is of benefit, interventions that involve behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy and ongoing surveillance is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough, new left-sided chest discomfort, and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg. He also reports increasing fatigue but no fever or infectious symptoms. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. Given the new symptoms in a lung cancer survivor, recurrence must be considered, while he also remains due for ongoing surveillance after curative-intent treatment.
recommended_actions: Evaluate the new cough, chest discomfort, weight loss, and fatigue according to symptoms suggestive of recurrence.
Provide surveillance follow-up care after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging.
Provide smoking cessation counselling.
generated_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because this lung cancer survivor has new symptoms suggestive of recurrence. R005 applies because he has completed curative-intent therapy, so surveillance follow-up care is required. R008 applies because ongoing surveillance should include medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging. R006 applies because smoking cessation counselling is recommended after curative-intent therapy for NSCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance reaches age 80.
selected_rules.execute: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
selected_rules.source_statement: Serial biopsy should not continue past the age of 80.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has low-risk localized prostate cancer (Gleason score ≤6).
selected_rules.execute: Use active surveillance as the preferred disease management strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with low-risk (Gleason score ≤6) localized prostate cancer, AS is the preferred disease management strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include PSA testing every 3 to 6 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -PSA test every 3 to 6 months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include digital rectal examination every year.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -DRE every year.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Perform a 12- to 14-core confirmatory TRUS biopsy including anterior directed cores within 6 to 12 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance after confirmatory biopsy.
selected_rules.execute: Repeat serial biopsy at least every 3 to 5 years.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, or very low PSA free/total ratio.
selected_rules.execute: Investigate further with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Discordant findings between a patient's clinical course and pathologic findings can include rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, and very low PSA free/total ratio. Presence of these findings requires further investigation with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has discordant clinical findings and pathologic findings.
selected_rules.execute: Use mpMRI at entry or during follow-up when indicated.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, being cognizant of both the high cost of mpMRI and its promise, it is recommended that when a patient's clinical findings are discordant with the pathologic findings, a mpMRI is indicated. When indicated, it may be considered at entry or during follow-up.

generated_case.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been managed with active surveillance. His PSA has been stable around 5.1 ng/mL, his digital rectal exams have remained normal, and his confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading. He feels well and asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no new urinary symptoms and no concerning changes in PSA kinetics or exam findings.
recommended_actions: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that serial biopsy should not continue once a patient on active surveillance reaches age 80. No additional discordant findings are present to trigger imaging or earlier repeat biopsy recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document contains only publication/contact and copyright information, with no clinical recommendations.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.

selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. At the end of the note, the document includes only publication details and contact information for the lead author, Dr. Eric Winquist, through the PEBC, along with a statement that the evidence review has been published as a supplement. It also notes that the report and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. No diagnostic algorithm, treatment recommendation, or follow-up plan is provided in the document excerpt.
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as routine primary therapy with chemotherapy in this population
selected_rules.execute: Do not use G-CSF routinely as primary therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as primary therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering a fractionated weekly CHOP schedule instead of standard CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Use standard CHOP rather than fractionated weekly CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No benefits from fractionating the treatment schedule have been detected; the potential for outcomes to be inferior is suggested.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide in a compressed 14-day schedule
selected_rules.execute: Avoid CHOEP-14 because of excessive toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: patients receiving CHOEP every 14 days experienced excessive toxicity

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide versus CHOP alone in older patients
selected_rules.execute: Do not add etoposide routinely to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No differences were detected in response rate, TTF, or OS, and the authors commented that the addition of etoposide was associated with significant toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering more intensive regimens than CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Avoid more intensive regimens when they add toxicity without survival benefit
selected_rules.source_statement: Two studies testing chemotherapy regimens that are more intensive than CHOP both detected an increase in toxicity with no survival benefit detected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering second- or third-generation regimens instead of CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Prefer CHOP because it is at least as effective or less toxic
selected_rules.source_statement: Subset analyses comparing CHOP with "second and third generation" regimens show that CHOP is at least as effective or less toxic than these other regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 60 with newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbid illness
selected_rules.execute: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine and prednisone (CHOP) is recommended for patients with no apparent cardiac disease or significant comorbidity. Dose and schedule should be the same as that used in younger patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and meets the guideline target population
selected_rules.execute: Add rituximab to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to CHOP is recommended for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension but no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure.
recommended_actions: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
Add rituximab to CHOP.
generated_case.rationale: This patient fits the guideline target population: older than 60, newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbidity. For this group, CHOP is recommended at the same dose and schedule as in younger patients. Because he has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, rituximab should be added to CHOP.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HIV-related lymphoma and CD4 count below 50/mm3
selected_rules.execute: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to chemotherapy in patients with CD4 <50/mm 3 is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated HIV-related lymphoma, CD4 count at least 50/mm3, and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with HIV-related lymphoma with a CD4 count ≥50/mm 3 who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy, should receive their chemotherapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, no prior rituximab, and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have not previously received rituximab and who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy should receive this chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, prior rituximab exposure, response lasted at least one year, and candidate for therapy
selected_rules.execute: Give therapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have previously received rituximab ... and who have achieved a response of at least one year's duration from the last rituximab administration and who are appropriate candidates for therapy should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and response to combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab
selected_rules.execute: Follow treatment with maintenance rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding SLL, who respond to treatment with combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab, this treatment should be followed by the use of maintenance rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any patient planned for rituximab treatment
selected_rules.execute: Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: The Hematology Disease Site Group recommends that all patients be screened for surface antigen for hepatitis B (HBsAg) and for hepatitis B core antibody (HBcAb) prior to treatment with rituximab.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 34/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease but no CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
recommended_actions: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because the patient has HIV-related lymphoma and a CD4 count below 50/mm3. Under this rule, rituximab should not be added to chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with relapsed SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Consider second-line chemotherapy only after weighing treatment-free interval, response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity, and performance status.
selected_rules.source_statement: The selection of patients for treatment with second-line therapy should be dependent on the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and the performance status of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
selected_rules.source_statement: patients who relapse three or more months following the completion of first-line chemotherapy may benefit from retreatment with the same regimen that induced their initial response. This would generally mean retreatment with etoposide-cisplatin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider alternative regimens such as CAV or carboplatin plus etoposide.
selected_rules.source_statement: Alternative regimens may include cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and vincristine (CAV) or carboplatin and etoposide.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient initially responded to chemotherapy and response duration was 45 days or longer
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral topotecan is a possible alternative for patients who initially responded to chemotherapy and had a response duration of 45 days or longer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is not suitable for intravenous therapy and topotecan is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan because it is more convenient and may be an option when IV therapy is unsuitable.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is more convenient and may be a treatment option for patients not suitable for intravenous therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given orally
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 diarrhea.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given intravenously
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 neutropenia.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient fails to respond to first-line therapy or relapses shortly after first-line therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider enrollment in a clinical trial to help determine the optimal treatment regimen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinical trials are needed to determine the optimal treatment regimen.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 5 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just over 5 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks about options. Because he previously responded and the relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of chemotherapy, retreatment with the same regimen is being considered, along with other second-line options. Oral topotecan is also discussed as an alternative, since he responded initially and his response lasted longer than 45 days.
recommended_actions: Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
Because relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of first-line chemotherapy, consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Because he initially responded and the response duration was longer than 45 days, consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette meets the activation conditions for relapsed SCLC, relapse more than 3 months after first-line chemotherapy, and prior response lasting at least 45 days. The recommendations are limited to those specified by the triggered rules and reflect the need to balance treatment-free interval, prior response, residual toxicity, and performance status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with inoperable, advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent systemic treatment strategies should be part of an informed discussion of treatment options for this group of patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer wants a break from treatment
selected_rules.execute: Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who want a break from treatment can be reassured that intermittent strategies of administering first-line therapy are a reasonable alternative to continuous administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Meta-analysis demonstrates no clinically significant survival difference between the continuous and intermittent chemotherapy strategies (HR, 1.02; 95%CI, 0.95-1.10, p=0.62).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent strategies of administering first-line systemic therapies to patients with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) do not result in a statistically significant reduction in overall survival and either improve or maintain quality of life compared to continuous administration of therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is receiving intermittent systemic therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval
selected_rules.execute: Continue best supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: During maintenance therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval, best supportive care should be continued for patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment as a reasonable guideline to consider
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Monitor with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Disease progresses during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.execute: Reintroduce the induction chemotherapy at disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line systemic treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in.
recommended_actions: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making.
Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy.
Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy.
Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with inoperable advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy, and he also explicitly wants a break from treatment. The selected rules support discussing and reassuring about intermittent therapy, noting no statistically significant overall survival reduction and potential quality-of-life benefit versus continuous therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has never been sexually active
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is younger than 21 years old
selected_rules.execute: Do not screen routinely.
selected_rules.source_statement: the authors of this guideline have concluded that the harms of screening women under 21 years of age significantly outweigh the benefits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: organized screening program with adequate database infrastructure
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV DNA testing of cervical cells for primary cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV DNA testing of cells collected from the cervix is recommended for primary cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive HPV DNA test result
selected_rules.execute: Perform cytology screening as triage rather than using cytology as the primary screen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Cytology screening, which was recommended for primary screening in the previous version of this guideline, is now recommended only in the event of a positive HPV DNA test result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 years of age or older
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV-based primary screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: the primary recommendations presented in Part 1 of this guideline are for HPVbased testing for women 30 years of age and over.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 to 65 years old and initial HPV test is negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV DNA testing at five-year intervals.
selected_rules.source_statement: For women aged 30-65, HPV DNA testing is to occur at five-year intervals after an initial negative result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV test is positive
selected_rules.execute: Assess the result with cytology testing and do not refer directly to colposcopy.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV-positive tests should be assessed with cytology testing and not referred directly to colposcopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV positive and cytology negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV testing after one year.
selected_rules.source_statement: Repeat HPV testing for results of HPV positive/cytology negative should be conducted after one year.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. After discussing her preventive care questions, she specifically asks whether cervical cancer screening is needed now.
recommended_actions: Do not perform cervical screening.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening. The recommended action is therefore to avoid cervical screening in this patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: inflammatory breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: BCS is not advised in inflammatory breast cancer because the extent of tumour involvement cannot be reliably ascertained.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sentinel lymph node biopsy is being considered both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform SLNB both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This suggests that SLNB should not be performed both before and after NACT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: cases being discussed for treatment planning
selected_rules.execute: Discuss cases in a multidisciplinary case conference.
selected_rules.source_statement: Communication between oncologists, surgeons, radiologists, and pathologists is essential. A multidisciplinary case conference is the recommended forum for discussion of cases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: specimen submitted after neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Indicate any prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission.
selected_rules.source_statement: Any prior use of neoadjuvant therapy should be indicated when specimens are submitted for pathologic examination.

selected_rules.activation_condition: before starting neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location.
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that surgical clips marking the original (pretreatment) tumour location be inserted before administration of neoadjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer and surgery is being planned after response to neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: For most patients with LABC, mastectomy should be considered to be the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient strongly desires breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: Perform wide excision of the remaining tumour in the original tumour bed plus radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Wide excision of the remaining tumour in the region of the original pre-neoadjuvant treatment tumour bed plus RT is recommended for patients with LABC who strongly desire BCS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer after mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Give postoperative radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiotherapy following mastectomy is recommended for patients with LABC.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving more than one-third of the breast, consistent with inflammatory breast cancer. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options, and she asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is also planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. No surgery has been performed yet.
recommended_actions: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette describes inflammatory breast cancer, which triggers the recommendation against breast-conserving surgery because tumour extent cannot be reliably ascertained. It also includes locally advanced breast cancer being managed with neoadjuvant therapy and later surgery planning, which supports considering mastectomy as standard of care for most such patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR2 or greater, including refractory or relapsed disease
selected_rules.execute: Perform allo-SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Allo-SCT is recommended in CR2 or greater (refractory or relapsed).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in first complete remission (CR1) and eligible for allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) is an option for adult patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in first complete remission (CR1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is deemed unsuitable for standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC)
selected_rules.execute: Use reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) is an option for patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in remission when they are deemed unsuitable for the standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC) regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is ineligible for MAC allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC as an effective therapeutic option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the evidence, the members of the Working Group have determined that RIC could be an effective therapeutic option for patients with ALL who are ineligible for MAC allo-SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient with high risk of treatment-related mortality from MAC
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC instead of MAC
selected_rules.source_statement: The systematic review (4) stated that RIC may be a potential therapeutic option in patients with high risk of treatment-related mortality (TRM) associated with MAC regimens, as there was a lack of overall survival benefit of MAC over RIC regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) after allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: Post-transplant use of a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) is a reasonable option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Ph+ ALL undergoing allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider TKI therapy pre- and/or post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: The consensus is that TKI therapy is useful pre and/or posttransplant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR1 or later who lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor
selected_rules.execute: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (haplo-SCT)
selected_rules.source_statement: Haploidentical hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (haplo-SCT) for patients with ALL in CR1 or later who lack a suitable related or unrelated donor is a reasonable option.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case meets criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
recommended_actions: Perform allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT).
generated_case.rationale: The vignette matches adult ALL in CR2 after relapse, which directly triggers the recommendation for allo-SCT. No additional rules are activated because the patient is not described as Ph+ ALL, unsuitable for myeloablative conditioning, or lacking a suitable donor.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient intends postoperative radiation and has lymph node positive early breast cancer, inflammatory breast cancer, or locally advanced breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: early breast cancer who are lymph node positive, inflammatory breast cancer or locally advanced breast cancer who will require postoperative RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Invasive tumor involves the skin or nipple-areolar complex clinically
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: any clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex (NAC) involvement by invasive tumour

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has Paget disease of the breast or a retroareolar tumor
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: NSM and ASM are not recommended for women with Paget disease of the breast or women with a retroareolar tumour.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Postmastectomy breast reconstruction has been performed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surveillance mammography routinely in the reconstructed breast
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of postmastectomy surveillance mammography in the reconstructed breast.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At the time mastectomy is offered for therapeutic mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: The discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction should be initiated at the time that mastectomy is offered by the general surgeon.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for DCIS
selected_rules.execute: Perform preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon should be performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for early stage breast cancer and adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy may be needed
selected_rules.execute: Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in preoperative evaluation, either by formal consultation or multidisciplinary cancer conference
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for early stage breast cancer who will potentially require adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy (RT), a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist should be included in preoperative evaluation, either through a formal consultation or by a multidisciplinary cancer conference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate reconstruction is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate reconstruction, there should be adequate preoperative imaging of the breasts, aligning with existing guideline recommendations.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and is expected to receive postoperative radiation, the surgical team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical, reconstructive, and radiation planning before finalizing the operative approach.
recommended_actions: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy because she intends to receive postoperative radiation and has lymph node-positive early breast cancer.
Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.
generated_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient has lymph node-positive early breast cancer and will receive postoperative radiation, making skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, and areola-sparing mastectomy not recommended. R005 applies because mastectomy is being offered, so reconstruction should be discussed. R007 applies because immediate reconstruction is being considered and adjuvant radiotherapy is likely, so oncology input should be included preoperatively. R008 applies because immediate reconstruction is being planned and adequate preoperative breast imaging is required.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering limited ACP implementation
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
selected_rules.source_statement: Addressing only one, or a few, of these elements creates the risks of leaving critical gaps that could prevent positive impacts on quality patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using education alone to improve ACP care
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect education alone to improve care or patient experience without supporting system changes.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, high-quality education, although essential, on its own is unlikely to improve care or the patient experience in the absence of supporting systemic changes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering whether pediatric ACP causes distress
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
selected_rules.source_statement: Concerns that ACP discussions will cause distress in children and parents are not supported by the evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering ACP or GoCD tools for practice
selected_rules.execute: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
selected_rules.source_statement: Outcome evidence for ACP suggests the greatest impact is likely to be made by a system-wide approach to integrating ACP into practice, one that maximizes clinical skills of all interprofessional team members.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When developing ACP tools or processes
selected_rules.execute: Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
selected_rules.source_statement: Considering these emerging themes from the evidence suggests that tool development should focus on enabling healthcare providers to facilitate values-based discussions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When evaluating ACP tools
selected_rules.execute: Use values-based outcome measures to assess impact on patient care.
selected_rules.source_statement: Future studies with corresponding valuesbased outcome measures are needed to evaluate the impact on patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When implementing ACP programs
selected_rules.execute: Include community engagement, professional education, standard practices, quality improvement monitoring, supporting policies, and technology infrastructure.
selected_rules.source_statement: A comprehensive approach to ACP includes community engagement, professional education, development of standard practices, monitoring of these practices through Quality Improvement initiatives, supporting policies and practices, and a technology infrastructures.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning ACP implementation across care settings
selected_rules.execute: Apply ACP across public health and healthcare sectors with system-level coordination and planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: This approach must be applied across public health and healthcare sectors to support the exchange of clinical information and will involve system-level coordination and planning.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient ACP workflow and realizes that a few isolated steps have not changed care quality in the past.
recommended_actions: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
generated_case.rationale: The vignette describes limited, fragmented ACP implementation, which triggers the warning against relying on only a few ACP elements. It also includes concern that ACP discussions may distress the patient and family, matching the rule that evidence does not support assuming increased distress. The team is planning broader ACP workflow changes, which supports a system-wide approach, and the patient’s stated priorities point to values-based rather than treatment-only discussions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria
selected_rules.execute: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Ineligible patients include those who have received >2 prior anticancer regimens or who had refractory disease, patients with a history of bowel obstruction (including subocclusive disease) related to underlying disease, a history of abdominal fistula, gastrointestinal perforation, or intra-abdominal abscess, or evidence of rectosigmoid involvement by pelvic examination, bowel involvement on computed tomography, or clinical symptoms of bowel obstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Assess each patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference; recognize systemic therapy is not curative.
selected_rules.source_statement: Systemic therapy for recurrent ovarian cancer is not curative. As such, it is recognized that, to determine the optimal therapy, each patient needs to be assessed individually in terms of recurrence, sensitivity to platinum, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer participation in clinical trials if appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: All patients should be offered the opportunity to participate in clinical trials, if appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer and a single platinum agent is not being considered because of toxicity or allergy
selected_rules.execute: Use monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin.
selected_rules.source_statement: If a single platinum agent is not being considered (e.g., because of toxicity or allergy), then monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin is a reasonable treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer with known BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, somatic or germline, after platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PARP inhibitor maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: PARP inhibitors are recommended for patients with known BRCA 1 or 2 mutation (somatic and germline) as maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Set treatment goals to improving quality of life by extending symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or prolonging life if possible.
selected_rules.source_statement: Lower levels of response to treatment are expected for this group; therefore, the goals of treatment should be to improve patient's quality of life by extending the symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or if possible, prolonging life.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, and gemcitabine have demonstrated activity in this patient population and are reasonable treatment options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer chemotherapy with biologics after discussing the safety profile.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer should be offered chemotherapy with biologics after a discussion concerning the safety profile

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with symptomatic recurrence 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens and now has increasing abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and a CT scan showing peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. Her disease is therefore platinum-resistant and she is not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria because she has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. She asks about the next treatment steps and whether the goal is cure.
recommended_actions: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
Set treatment goals around improving quality of life, symptom control, and prolonging progression-free interval or life if possible.
Consider single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Assess the patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference, recognizing that systemic therapy is not curative.
Offer participation in a clinical trial if appropriate.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette matches platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and specifically excludes AURELIA eligibility because the patient has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. That triggers the recommendation not to use the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy option for AURELIA-eligible patients. Platinum-resistant disease also supports setting palliative goals and using one of the listed single-agent chemotherapy options. Because she has recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer, individualized treatment assessment and trial consideration are also recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced stage or recurrent endometrial cancer excluding sarcomas and squamous cell carcinomas
selected_rules.execute: use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy is favoured over single agent chemotherapy because of higher response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: Paclitaxel in combination with cisplatin/doxorubicin chemotherapy improves both response rate and median survival; however, the use of this three-drug combination is associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer and minimal symptoms or non-life threatening disease
selected_rules.execute: consider hormonal therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Hormonal therapy may be a therapeutic option for those patients with minimal symptoms or non-life threatening advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer when choosing between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and doxorubicin/cisplatin
selected_rules.execute: weigh greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The decision to use the three-drug combination, consisting of cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel, should be made with consideration of both the greater toxicity and the three-month increase in median survival time in comparison with the two-drug doxorubicin/cisplatin regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: However, recent data suggest no benefit to the three-drug combination in terms of recurrence-free survival and was associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%, other agents in combination with doxorubicin reported response rates of 30% (cyclophosphamide) and 43% (paclitaxel).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent chemotherapy has reported response rates as follows: doxorubicin 17-27% and platinum agents 21%.

generated_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with progressive vaginal bleeding, pelvic discomfort, and new pulmonary nodules on surveillance CT, consistent with recurrent advanced disease. She remains ambulatory and has good renal function and performance status, but she is seeking treatment that offers the best chance of tumor shrinkage. Her oncology team discusses systemic therapy options, including single-agent and combination chemotherapy. Because her disease is advanced/recurrent and she is fit for treatment, the team considers multi-agent regimens rather than single-agent therapy. They also review the potential benefits and increased toxicity of a paclitaxel-containing three-drug regimen versus a two-drug comparator.
recommended_actions: Use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy.
Consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity.
Weigh the greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel regimen.
Note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity.
Consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%.
Consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%.
Consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates.
generated_case.rationale: This vignette fits advanced/recurrent endometrial cancer in an adult and triggers the chemotherapy selection rules. The patient is symptomatic and fit for systemic treatment, so combination chemotherapy is favored over single-agent therapy. The vignette also supports discussion of paclitaxel-containing combinations, the toxicity-survival tradeoff of the three-drug regimen, and comparator response-rate data for alternative regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma that does not overexpress HER2 and first-line chemotherapy is being given
selected_rules.execute: Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and HER2 is not overexpressed
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe a biological agent with first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Advanced gastric or GEJ carcinoma and later-line therapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor in addition to standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with gastric or GEJ carcinoma undergoing later lines of therapy, medical oncologists should not prescribe ICI in addition to standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult with advanced, non-resectable gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Recurrent or metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add an immune checkpoint inhibitor to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe the addition of trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen with an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.
